EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a weak high retreating over the northeast and deepening low pressure over the Great Lakes resulted in a significant accumulation of ice across portions of interior Northern New Jersey from the evening of January 6 into the afternoon of January 7. A warm front extending east from the developing low worked northward from the Mid Atlantic states with overrunning precipitation to its north.||Ice amounts averaged a little more than one-half inch across the western half of Passaic county, with the highest amount of 0.80 inches reported at Awosting.
Thunderstorms developed ahead of a warm front, resulting in numerous lightning strikes across northeastern portions of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outages were reported in River Edge due to downed power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across north central New Jersey, spawning numerous thunderstorms across the region. Cold air aloft resulted in the production of hail producing storms. Many reports of pea to dime size hail were received. Penny size hail was recorded in Bergen County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Ho-Ho-Kus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms formed along the warm frontal boundary in northern New Jersey during the late afternoon of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter size hail fell in Phillipsburg and Alpha.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed along a stationary front draped across the local area. This caused the Ho-Ho-Kus Brook at Ho-Ho-Kus and the Mahwah River at Suffern to slightly exceed their respective flood stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mahwah River at Suffern exceeded its flood stage of 4.0 ft. at 7:30am, crested at 4.11 ft. at 8am, and fell below flood stage at 9:15am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed along a stationary front draped across the local area. This caused the Ho-Ho-Kus Brook at Ho-Ho-Kus and the Mahwah River at Suffern to slightly exceed their respective flood stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The HoHoKus Brook at HoHoKus exceeded its flood stage of 3.0 ft. at 4:30am, crested to 3.06 ft. at 5:00 am, and fell below flood stage at 5:30am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow southward moving cold front located over the Philadelphia Delaware Valley during the morning of June 3rd helped trigger severe thunderstorms in southern New Jersey during that afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and wires in Pennsville Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes on the 9th produced a strong southerly flow of moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean. Rain, with some snow at the start, began falling during the late evening of the 8th and ended by Noon EST on the 9th across central and southern New Jersey. The rain fell heavy at times between 5 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST on the 9th. Event precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches. The concentration of the heavy rain and some headwater snow melt in a relatively short period of time produced poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of some of the streams and creeks in central and southern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Event precipitation totals included 2.15 inches in North Brunswick, 2.11 inches in New Brusnwick, 2.05 inches in South Brunswick, 1.77 inches in Cranbury, 1.71 inches in Monroe Township and 1.56 inches in Piscataway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same severe thunderstorm that produced large hail also produced estimated wind gusts up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful nor'easter produced periods of heavy rain along with the strong to high winds across southeastern New Jersey from the 11th through the 14th. While rain began falling on the 11th, the heaviest rain fell on the 12th into the first half of the 13th. The rain ended on the 14th. Event precipitation totals averaged 2 to 5 inches and produced some poor drainage and low lying area flooding. Runoff from streams, creeks and rivers were slowed by the higher than normal tides. The heavy rain also exacerbated the poor drainage and tidal flooding when it coincided with the high tides on the 12th.||Event precipitation totals included 5.50 inches at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County), 3.22 inches in Linwood (Atlantic County), 2.69 inches in Sea Isle City (Cape May County), 2.56 inches in Stafford Township (Ocean County), 2.52 inches in Upper Township (Cape May County), 2.41 inches in Lower Township and Woodbine (Cape May County), 2.24 inches in Middle Township (Cape May County), 2.17 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County), 1.89 inches in Folsom (Atlantic County) and 1.79 inches in Buena Vista (Atlantic County).| |The nor'easter was originally Hurricane Ida, which made landfall in Alabama on the morning of the 10th. As Ida made landfall, it lost tropical characteristics and transformed into an extratropical low pressure system. On the morning of November 11th, the low pressure system reached southern Georgia and the next morning (12th) it was located over the coastal waters east of Wilmington, North Carolina. The extratropical low pressure system reached its maximum strength of around 995 millibars during the evening of the 12th as it was located just off of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina. From there, the low pressure system slowly drifted off the North Carolina coast as it slowly weakened on the 13th and 14th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Saddle River at Lodi rose above its flood stage of 6.0 ft at 7:15am EST.  The river crested at 6.16 ft. at 9:15am, and returned within its banks at 10:30am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two days of persistent strong westerly offshore flow resulted in blow out tides during the evening low tide on the 11th along the Atlantic Coast and the overnight low tide in Delaware Bay and the lower Delaware River. The low tide reached 2.11 feet below mean lower low water in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 2.53 feet feet below mean lower low water at Barnegat Light (Ocean County), 2.19 feet feet below mean lower low water in Avalon (Cape May County), 1.84 feet feet below mean lower low water in Stone Harbor (Cape May County), 2.26 feet below mean lower low water in Cape May (Cape May County) and 1.86 feet below mean lower low water at Reedy Island (New Castle County, Delaware). Low water problems with blowout tides start at 1.8 feet below mean lower low water.||The blow out tides were caused by the persistent westerly wind flow between a low pressure system over eastern Canada and a high pressure system over the southern United States. As the high pressure system neared the region on the 12th, winds diminished and tides returned to closer to normal levels.

A strong and persistent east northeast wind gradient existed over the region between strong low pressure off the Carolinas (remnants of Ida) and a strong high pressure system located over Eastern Canada between November 11th and the 14th.  The tidal piling over several tidal cycles caused widespread minor to locally moderate coastal flooding along New York Harbor and the Hackensack River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 7 was closed along the Hackensack River due to moderate tidal flooding.

As Ida made landfall, it lost tropical characteristics and transformed into an extratropical low pressure system. On the morning of November 11th, the low pressure system reached southern Georgia and the next morning (12th) it was located over the coastal waters east of Wilmington, North Carolina. The extratropical low pressure system reached its maximum strength of around 995 millibars during the evening of the 12th as it was located just off of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina. From there, the low pressure system slowly drifted off the North Carolina coast as it slowly weakened on the 13th and 14th.

Strong south winds preceded the arrival of a cold front in New Jersey during the afternoon of the 28th. Peak wind gusts averaged between 30 and 40 mph. In Pine Beach (Ocean County), wires were pulled down by the gusty winds.
A northward moving warm front during the morning of the 9th, produced showers with heavy rain. This caused some poor drainage flooding in Camden County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers with heavy rain caused the flooding of underpasses on U.S. Route 130 in Pennsauken Township. The roadway was closed until the flooding receded. A Skywarn spotter measured 2.25 inches in Pennsauken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching pre-frontal trough along with a passing upper level disturbance caused a squall line to form and move across Northeastern New Jersey. The line caused wind damage across Passaic and Bergen Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were reported down across Paramus Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds occurred on the 29th after a cold front moved through New Jersey during the early morning hours. Winds decreased during the second half of the afternoon after a high pressure system from the Midwest moved closer to the region. Peak wind gusts occurred during the morning, averaged around 50 mph and knocked down some weak trees, tree limbs and wires and caused scattered outages.||A peak wind gust of 63 mph was measured at the top of the High Point Monument in Sussex County. Other peak wind gusts included 56 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 54 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), 53 mph in Seaside Heights (Ocean County), 52 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 47 mph in Ringoes (Hunterdon County), Harvey Cedars (Ocean County) and the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County), 46 mph in Somerville (Somerset County), 45 mph in Trenton (Mercer County) and Woodstown (Salem County) and 44 mph in Belmar (Monmouth County) and Wrightstown (Burlington County).

Very strong south winds mixed to the ground after a warm front moved through eastern New Jersey during the early morning of the 3rd. Peak wind gust averaged around 50 mph and tore down some weak tree limbs, trees and wires. The strong winds also caused some minor tidal flooding in Monmouth and Middlesex Counties on the morning of the 3rd. ||Peak wind gusts included 67 mph at Cape May Harbor (Cape May County), 59 mph at the Marina within Atlantic City (Atlantic County), 52 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 48 mph in Kit Island (Ocean County), 47 mph in West Cape May (Cape May County) and Tuckerton (Ocean County), 45 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and Woodbine (Cape May County) and 43 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water.

Moderate tidal flooding was caused by a strong pressure gradient between high pressure to the north and a coastal storm passing south of Long Island Thursday night October 15th and Friday, October 16th.||A prolonged period of strong east to northeast winds across coastal waters coupled with astronomically high tides caused water to build along the coast followed by tidal piling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 7 in Kearney was reported closed due to coastal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system associated with low pressure moving through the Great Lakes region, produced rainfall over a period of 18 to 24 hours which caused flooding in parts of Bergen County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The railroad underpass at Franklin Avenue was closed by local authorities due to flooding with 2 cars stranded in high water.  The intersection of Ackerman Ave and Delmar Ave. was also closed due to flooding.  Water from street flooding entered a house and garage at 607 Prospect St. in Glen Rock. Total rainfall across Bergen County ranged from 1.83 inches in Mahwah to 2.05 inches in Teterboro and Ridgewood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level short wave spawned an isolated severe thunderstorm which moved through Hudson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail was reported in Hoboken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front and leftover moisture in the area gave the region a second round of wintry weather during the evening of the 31st and the early morning of January 1st. Snow mainly fell in Sussex, Warren and Morris Counties where additional accumulations were around an inch or less. In Hunterdon and Somerset Counties, a wintry mix of mainly sleet and freezing rain fell. Ice accretions were less than one tenth of an inch, but any untreated walkways and roadways were hazardous. The precipitation spread from south to north between 5 p.m. and 8 p.m. EST on the 31st and ended between 2 a.m. and 4 a.m. EST on January 1st.

An approaching cold front and leftover moisture in the area gave the region a second round of wintry weather during the evening of the 31st and the early morning of January 1st. Snow mainly fell in Sussex, Warren and Morris Counties where additional accumulations were around an inch or less. In Hunterdon and Somerset Counties, a wintry mix of mainly sleet and freezing rain fell. Ice accretions were less than one tenth of an inch, but any untreated walkways and roadways were hazardous. The precipitation spread from south to north between 5 p.m. and 8 p.m. EST on the 31st and ended between 2 a.m. and 4 a.m. EST on January 1st.

Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water.

Coastal low pressure rapidly intensified Saturday night as it tracked northeast from off the Delmarva Coast...just southeast of the 40N/70W benchmark���and to southeast of Cape Cod by Sunday morning. This was in response to a powerful upper low approaching and then crossing south of the area. The track of low pressure to the southeast of the region and stubborn placement of arctic high pressure over the Northern US Plains and Southern Canadian Plains...ridging eastward...made for the ideal setup for heavy snow across most of the region. The rapid intensification of low pressure created widespread blowing and drifting of snow...particularly over eastern portions of the region...with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour and northeast winds of 15 to 25 mph and gusts 30 to 40 mph during the height of the storm. Snow totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches across the region...with around 1 ft across the NJ metro area.

Sleet and mainly freezing rain occurred over the higher terrain of Sussex County from the evening of the 25th into the morning of the 26th. Steady precipitation began during the evening on the 25th as a wintry mix over the higher terrain, but changed to freezing rain quickly. Freezing rain then persisted through the night and changed over to plain rain by around 7 a.m. EST on the 26th. Ice accretions averaged around one quarter of an inch. Any untreated walkways and roadways were hazardous.||The freezing rain was caused by a surge of warm air, first aloft, arriving from the south around an intense low pressure system that was centered over the Upper Mississippi Valley. While the warm air arrived faster aloft, it lagged at the surface and was the cause for the freezing rain until temperatures even at the surface rose above freezing.

An intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes on the 9th produced a strong southerly flow of moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean. Rain, with some snow at the start, began falling during the late evening of the 8th and ended by Noon EST on the 9th across central and southern New Jersey. The rain fell heavy at times between 5 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST on the 9th. Event precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches. The concentration of the heavy rain and some headwater snow melt in a relatively short period of time produced poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of some of the streams and creeks in central and southern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Route 9 in Freehold was closed due to the heavy rain. Event precipitation totals included 3.04 inches in Eatontown, 2.35 inches in Fair Haven, 2.21 inches in Belmar, 2.20 inches in Sea Girt, 1.97 inches in Asbury Park, 1.91 inches in Shrewsbury Township, 1.85 inches in Freehold, 1.84 inches in Wall Township, 1.83 inches in Holmdel Township, 1.79 inches in Lake Como, 1.75 inches in Howell Township and 1.74 inches in Red Bank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southwest winds occurred during the day of the 9th preceding a cold frontal passage early in the evening across the southern half of New Jersey.  Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph and coupled with the heavy rain, helped knock down shallow rooted trees, weak tree limbs and wires. In Vineland (Cumberland County), a downed tree damaged a home. Atlantic City Electric reported about 1,700 homes and businesses lost power in southeast New Jersey during the height of the storm on the morning of the 9th. On December 10th and 11th, gusty west winds prevailed throughout New Jersey, but peak wind gusts were on average between 40 and 45 mph.  A few additional weak tree limbs and poles were knocked down. Peak wind gusts on the 9th included 53 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 52 mph in Seaside Heights (Ocean County) and 45 mph at the Marina within Atlantic City (Atlantic County).

An intense low pressure system brought a wintry mix of snow and sleet to northwest New Jersey during the early morning of the 9th before the precipitation changed over to rain. Accumulations averaged 2 to 7 inches with the highest amounts over the higher terrain of Hunterdon, Morris, Sussex and Warren Counties. ||Snow began during the late evening of the 8th as far south as Somerset and Hunterdon Counties. As warmer air moved north, the snow changed to rain by around 3 a.m. EST in those counties. Farther to the north, snow continued to fall and changed to rain as late as around 6 a.m. EST in Sussex County. During the transition from snow to rain, some sleet also fell. The rain ended by Noon EST on the 9th. Most schools in northwest New Jersey had two hour delayed openings. ||Specific snowfall included 7.5 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 7.0 inches in Sussex (Sussex County), 6.1 inches in Jefferson (Morris County), 5.6 inches in Marcella and Rockaway (Morris County), 5.2 inches in Hardyston (Sussex County), 5.1 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 5.0 inches in Lebanon and Tewksbury (Hunterdon County) and Knowlton (Warren County), 4.8 inches in Roxbury (Morris County), 4.3 inches in Denville (Morris County), 4.0 inches in Hamburg (Sussex County), 2.7 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 2.4 inches in Hope (Warren County) and 1.5 inches in High Bridge (Hunterdon County), Bernards (Somerset County), and Greenwich (Warren County). ||The wintry mix was caused by a strong surge of relatively warmer and more moist air above a surface warm front associated with an intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes. A weaker low pressure  system formed on the warm frontal boundary early in the morning of the 9th near the Virginia and North Carolina border. This slowed the advance of milder air into the higher terrain locations.

Freezing rain occurred across most of the Raritan Basin as well as all of northwest New Jersey from the morning into the afternoon of the 13th. Ice accretions averaged between one and three tenths and led to many accidents on untreated roadways, bridges and overpasses. Some sleet also fell in Sussex County. Freezing rain began between 9 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST. As warmer air moved in at the surface, the freezing rain changed to rain early in the afternoon in Hunterdon and Somerset Counties, but took most of the rest of the afternoon for it to occur elsewhere in northwest New Jersey. Precipitation ended around 6 p.m. EST.  ||Numerous accidents were reported on Interstate 80 in Morris and Warren Counties and along Interstate 78, Interstate 287 and New Jersey State Route 23 in Morris County. Between 10 a.m. EST and 2 p.m. EST, New Jersey State Police alone reported 200 accidents. There were overturned vehicles and vehicles in ditches. Five injuries were reported.  Parts of Interstate 80 were closed in Warren County. In all about fourteen miles of Interstate 80 were closed.|  |The freezing rain resulted from precipitation that formed ahead of an advancing surface warm front. A major winter storm dropped heavy snow across all of New Jersey except for Sussex County. The heaviest snow fell along and east of the Interstate 95 corridor.   Snowfall averaged around 3 inches in Sussex County, 6 to 12 inches across the rest of northwest New Jersey, the Raritan Basin and Cape May County and 12 to 24 inches (with some locally higher amounts) across the rest of central and southern New Jersey. The 23.2 inches of snow that fell at the Philadelphia International Airport was the second heaviest snowfall on record.||Snow began around 1 a.m. EST across extreme southern New Jersey, but took a long time to spread north. Snow began around 7 a.m. EST along the Interstate 195 corridor, around Noon EST in the Raritan Basin and during the afternoon in northwest New Jersey. The snow mixed with sleet at times during the day on the 19th in southeast New Jersey and changed to sleet for a while in Cape May County. The snow fell at its heaviest between 6 a.m. EST and 6 p.m. EST across the southern fourth of New Jersey, between 10 a.m. EST and 10 p.m. EST across the northern half of southern New Jersey and during the afternoon and early evening across the Raritan Basin and north of Interstate 195 in central New Jersey. The snow ended between 1 a.m. and 3 a.m. EST at both ends of the state, but lingered through 8 a.m. EST in the central third of the state. | |A train engineer was injured after his New Jersey Transit Train struck a transit bus that became stuck on the tracks in Pennsauken (Camden County). In Monmouth County, the weight of the snow caused the collapse of a sports center bubble. The weight of the snow also caused the collapse of a 35 foot awning at a home in Toms River (Ocean County).|The winter storm postponed many planned local and high school sporting events. Since this storm occurred on the weekend, the number of traffic accidents was down. Nevertheless, there were 77 reported accidents in Burlington County and accidents snarled traffic on Interstates 295 and 676 in Cameden County.  AAA Mid Atlantic said that the volume of calls for a Saturday was 64 percent higher than normal; many were stranded motorists. Strong south to southwest winds occurred during the day of the 9th preceding a cold frontal passage early in the evening across the southern half of New Jersey.  Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph and coupled with the heavy rain, helped knock down shallow rooted trees, weak tree limbs and wires. In Vineland (Cumberland County), a downed tree damaged a home. Atlantic City Electric reported about 1,700 homes and businesses lost power in southeast New Jersey during the height of the storm on the morning of the 9th. On December 10th and 11th, gusty west winds prevailed throughout New Jersey, but peak wind gusts were on average between 40 and 45 mph.  A few additional weak tree limbs and poles were knocked down. Peak wind gusts on the 9th included 53 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 52 mph in Seaside Heights (Ocean County) and 45 mph at the Marina within Atlantic City (Atlantic County).

The combination of melting snow and between 1.5 and 3.0 inches of rain produced field, poor drainage and widespread river flooding in New Jersey. Rain spread from south to north during the evening of the 25th and fell at a fairly steady rate. The heaviest rain fell during the afternoon of the 26th in the southern half of the state and the evening of the 26th in the northern half of the state. The rain ended during the evening of the 26th in the southern part of the state and the early morning of the 27th in the northern part of the state. ||The steady rain was caused by a strong southerly flow of air from the Gulf of Mexico around an intense low pressure system that spent much of the 25th and 26th in Iowa before weakening and drifting to the northeast. The mild southerly flow also quickly melted the large snow pack in the central and southern part of the state. The rain ended after this low's occluded front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Maurice River at Norma was above its 4 foot flood stage from 200 a.m EST on the 27th through 115 p.m. EST on the 30th. It crested at 4.73 feet at 445 p.m. EST on the 28th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raccoon Creek at Swedesboro was above its 13 foot flood stage from 445 p.m. EST on the 26th through 510 a.m. EST on the 27th. It crested at 13.39 feet at 1 a.m. EST on the 27th. Event precipitation totals were 2.56 inches in Franklin, 2.13 inches in Mantua and 1.75 inches in Washington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes on the 9th produced a strong southerly flow of moist air from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean. Rain, with some snow at the start, began falling during the late evening of the 8th and ended by Noon EST on the 9th across central and southern New Jersey. The rain fell heavy at times between 5 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST on the 9th. Event precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches. The concentration of the heavy rain and some headwater snow melt in a relatively short period of time produced poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of some of the streams and creeks in central and southern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the heavy rain produced flooding along sections of the Raccoon Creek during the afternoon and evening of the 9th in the county. The Raccoon Creek near Swedesboro was above its 13 foot flood stage from 1236 p.m. EDT through 830 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 13.67 feet at 345 p.m. EST. Several basements across the county were flooded. Event precipitation totals included 2.55 inches in Franklin, 2.12 inches in South Harrison, 1.96 inches in West Deptford and 1.74 inches in Woolwich Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the heavy rain produced flooding along sections of the Cooper River during the day on the 9th. The Cooper River near Haddonfield was above its 2.8 foot flood stage from 808 a.m. EST through 546 p.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 3.32 feet at 1215 p.m. EST. In Camden one vehicle became stuck in high water. Event precipitation totals included 2.25 inches in Winslow Township, 2.23 inches in Somerdale, 1.95 inches in Pennsauken, 1.94 inches in Sicklerville and 1.79 inches in Cherry Hill.

Strong south to southwest winds occurred during the day of the 9th preceding a cold frontal passage early in the evening across the southern half of New Jersey.  Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph and coupled with the heavy rain, helped knock down shallow rooted trees, weak tree limbs and wires. In Vineland (Cumberland County), a downed tree damaged a home. Atlantic City Electric reported about 1,700 homes and businesses lost power in southeast New Jersey during the height of the storm on the morning of the 9th. On December 10th and 11th, gusty west winds prevailed throughout New Jersey, but peak wind gusts were on average between 40 and 45 mph.  A few additional weak tree limbs and poles were knocked down. Peak wind gusts on the 9th included 53 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 52 mph in Seaside Heights (Ocean County) and 45 mph at the Marina within Atlantic City (Atlantic County).

Atlantic City Electric reported about 1,700 homes and businesses lost power in southeast New Jersey during the height of the storm on the morning of the 9th. On December 10th and 11th, gusty west winds prevailed throughout New Jersey, but peak wind gusts were on average between 40 and 45 mph.  A few additional weak tree limbs and poles were knocked down. Peak wind gusts on the 9th included 53 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 52 mph in Seaside Heights (Ocean County) and 45 mph at the Marina within Atlantic City (Atlantic County).

The combination of melting snow and between 1.5 and 3.0 inches of rain produced field, poor drainage and widespread river flooding in New Jersey. Rain spread from south to north during the evening of the 25th and fell at a fairly steady rate. The heaviest rain fell during the afternoon of the 26th in the southern half of the state and the evening of the 26th in the northern half of the state. The rain ended during the evening of the 26th in the southern part of the state and the early morning of the 27th in the northern part of the state. ||The steady rain was caused by a strong southerly flow of air from the Gulf of Mexico around an intense low pressure system that spent much of the 25th and 26th in Iowa before weakening and drifting to the northeast. The mild southerly flow also quickly melted the large snow pack in the central and southern part of the state. The rain ended after this low's occluded front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raritan River at Manville was above its 14 foot flood stage from 841 a.m. EST through 245 p.m. EST on the 27th. It crested at 14.53 feet at 1145 a.m. EST. Farther downstream the Raritan River at Bound Brook was above its 28 foot flood stage from 1125 a.m. EST through 615 p.m. EST on the 27th. It crested at 28.54 feet at 215 p.m. EST. The Millstone River at Griggstown was above its 10 foot flood stage from 415 p.m. EST on the 26th through 200 a.m. EST on the 29th. It crested at 14.90 feet at 945 a.m. EST on the 27th. Farther downstream, the Millstone River at Blackwells Mills was above its 9 foot flood stage from 153 a.m. EST on the 27th through 1040 a.m. EST on the 28th. It crested at 11.40 feet at 130 p.m. EST. The North Branch of the Raritan River at South Branch crested at 8.48 feet at 10 a.m. EST on the 27th. Flood stage is 7 feet. The times it went above and below flood stage were not available.||Event precipitation totals included 1.92 incheas in Skillman, 1.85 inches in Franklin, 1.75 inches in Montgomery, 1.73 inches in Pottersville, 1.44 inches in Bridgewater, 1.37 inches in Bernards and 1.21 inches in Hillsborough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system brought a wintry mix of snow and sleet to northwest New Jersey during the early morning of the 9th before the precipitation changed over to rain. Accumulations averaged 2 to 7 inches with the highest amounts over the higher terrain of Hunterdon, Morris, Sussex and Warren Counties. ||Snow began during the late evening of the 8th as far south as Somerset and Hunterdon Counties. As warmer air moved north, the snow changed to rain by around 3 a.m. EST in those counties. Farther to the north, snow continued to fall and changed to rain as late as around 6 a.m. EST in Sussex County. During the transition from snow to rain, some sleet also fell. The rain ended by Noon EST on the 9th. Most schools in northwest New Jersey had two hour delayed openings. ||Specific snowfall included 7.5 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 7.0 inches in Sussex (Sussex County), 6.1 inches in Jefferson (Morris County), 5.6 inches in Marcella and Rockaway (Morris County), 5.2 inches in Hardyston (Sussex County), 5.1 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 5.0 inches in Lebanon and Tewksbury (Hunterdon County) and Knowlton (Warren County), 4.8 inches in Roxbury (Morris County), 4.3 inches in Denville (Morris County), 4.0 inches in Hamburg (Sussex County), 2.7 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 2.4 inches in Hope (Warren County) and 1.5 inches in High Bridge (Hunterdon County), Bernards (Somerset County), and Greenwich (Warren County). ||The wintry mix was caused by a strong surge of relatively warmer and more moist air above a surface warm front associated with an intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes. A weaker low pressure  system formed on the warm frontal boundary early in the morning of the 9th near the Virginia and North Carolina border. This slowed the advance of milder air into the higher terrain locations.

A weak low pressure system dropped light snow across northern and southwestern New Jersey and a wintry mix across southeast New Jersey during the morning of 31st. Snowfall averaged 1 to 3 inches with ice accretions less than one tenth of an inch. Precipitation began as a wintry mix across southeast New Jersey and as snow across southwest New Jersey between 4 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST. The snow spread north through the rest of New Jersey by 7 a.m. EST. The wintry mix changed to plain rain around the middle of the morning in southeast New Jersey and continued to fall as light snow elsewhere. All precipitation ended by Noon EST.  The precipitation's timing coupled with high traffic volume resulted in numerous accidents. The majority of the reported accidents were in southeastern New Jersey where the frozen precipitation fell primarily as freezing rain.||The most serious accident occurred in Upper Township of Cape May County when a 39-year-old mother and 8-year-old daughter died after she lost control of her vehicle on a curve and collided with a propane truck. In Atlantic County, there were two dozen reported accidents on the Atlantic City Expressway that resulted in five injuries. The tunnel of the Atlantic City Expressway Connector was closed because of an accident. The County Route 152 Bridge between Longport and Somers Point was closed. In Burlington County, the State Police reported about 30 accidents on the Garden State Parkway. In Cumberland County, over three dozen accidents were reported including State Route 47. In Mercer County, 10 accidents were reported in Princeton including an injury when a 36-year-old woman's Sports Utility Vehicle rolled over. ||Specific snowfall included 2.4 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.0 inches in Parsippany (Morris County) and Blairstown (Warren County), 1.8 inches in Whitehouse Station (Hunterdon County) and Branchburg (Somerset County), 1.4 inches in Lindenwold (Camden County), 1.3 inches in Florence (Burlington County), 1.2 inches in Metuchen (Middlesex County) and Cream Ridge (Morris County), 1.1 inches in Ewing (Mercer County) and 0.2 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport. Ice accretions were one tenth of an inch or less.||The low pressure system that caused the wintry mix moved northeast from Mobile, Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the 31st and reached Wilmington, North Carolina at 1 p.m. EST and was southeast of Long Island at 7 p.m. EST that evening.

The snow spread north through the rest of New Jersey by 7 a.m. EST. The wintry mix changed to plain rain around the middle of the morning in southeast New Jersey and continued to fall as light snow elsewhere. All precipitation ended by Noon EST.  The precipitation's timing coupled with high traffic volume resulted in numerous accidents. The majority of the reported accidents were in southeastern New Jersey where the frozen precipitation fell primarily as freezing rain.||The most serious accident occurred in Upper Township of Cape May County when a 39-year-old mother and 8-year-old daughter died after she lost control of her vehicle on a curve and collided with a propane truck. In Atlantic County, there were two dozen reported accidents on the Atlantic City Expressway that resulted in five injuries. The tunnel of the Atlantic City Expressway Connector was closed because of an accident. The County Route 152 Bridge between Longport and Somers Point was closed. In Burlington County, the State Police reported about 30 accidents on the Garden State Parkway. In Cumberland County, over three dozen accidents were reported including State Route 47. In Mercer County, 10 accidents were reported in Princeton including an injury when a 36-year-old woman's Sports Utility Vehicle rolled over. ||Specific snowfall included 2.4 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.0 inches in Parsippany (Morris County) and Blairstown (Warren County), 1.8 inches in Whitehouse Station (Hunterdon County) and Branchburg (Somerset County), 1.4 inches in Lindenwold (Camden County), 1.3 inches in Florence (Burlington County), 1.2 inches in Metuchen (Middlesex County) and Cream Ridge (Morris County), 1.1 inches in Ewing (Mercer County) and 0.2 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport. Ice accretions were one tenth of an inch or less.||The low pressure system that caused the wintry mix moved northeast from Mobile, Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the 31st and reached Wilmington, North Carolina at 1 p.m. EST and was southeast of Long Island at 7 p.m. EST that evening.

Strong south to southwest winds occurred during the day of the 9th preceding a cold frontal passage early in the evening across the southern half of New Jersey.  Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph and coupled with the heavy rain, helped knock down shallow rooted trees, weak tree limbs and wires. In Vineland (Cumberland County), a downed tree damaged a home. Atlantic City Electric reported about 1,700 homes and businesses lost power in southeast New Jersey during the height of the storm on the morning of the 9th. On December 10th and 11th, gusty west winds prevailed throughout New Jersey, but peak wind gusts were on average between 40 and 45 mph.  A few additional weak tree limbs and poles were knocked down. Peak wind gusts on the 9th included 53 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 52 mph in Seaside Heights (Ocean County) and 45 mph at the Marina within Atlantic City (Atlantic County).

The combination of melting snow and between 1.5 and 3.0 inches of rain produced field, poor drainage and widespread river flooding in New Jersey. Rain spread from south to north during the evening of the 25th and fell at a fairly steady rate. The heaviest rain fell during the afternoon of the 26th in the southern half of the state and the evening of the 26th in the northern half of the state. The rain ended during the evening of the 26th in the southern part of the state and the early morning of the 27th in the northern part of the state. ||The steady rain was caused by a strong southerly flow of air from the Gulf of Mexico around an intense low pressure system that spent much of the 25th and 26th in Iowa before weakening and drifting to the northeast. The mild southerly flow also quickly melted the large snow pack in the central and southern part of the state. The rain ended after this low's occluded front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Event precipitation totals included 1.90 inches in South Brunswick, 1.87 inches in North Brunswick, 1.81 inches in New Brunswick and 1.66 inches in Cranbury.

Event precipitation totals included 2.95 inches in Colts Neck, 2.82 inches in Freehold, 2.81 inches in Howell, 2.33 inches in Middletown, 2.20 inches in Wall, 1.28 inches in Lake Como and 1.17 inches in Shrewsbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system brought a wintry mix of snow and sleet to northwest New Jersey during the early morning of the 9th before the precipitation changed over to rain. Accumulations averaged 2 to 7 inches with the highest amounts over the higher terrain of Hunterdon, Morris, Sussex and Warren Counties. ||Snow began during the late evening of the 8th as far south as Somerset and Hunterdon Counties. As warmer air moved north, the snow changed to rain by around 3 a.m. EST in those counties. Farther to the north, snow continued to fall and changed to rain as late as around 6 a.m. EST in Sussex County. During the transition from snow to rain, some sleet also fell. The rain ended by Noon EST on the 9th. Most schools in northwest New Jersey had two hour delayed openings. ||Specific snowfall included 7.5 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 7.0 inches in Sussex (Sussex County), 6.1 inches in Jefferson (Morris County), 5.6 inches in Marcella and Rockaway (Morris County), 5.2 inches in Hardyston (Sussex County), 5.1 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 5.0 inches in Lebanon and Tewksbury (Hunterdon County) and Knowlton (Warren County), 4.8 inches in Roxbury (Morris County), 4.3 inches in Denville (Morris County), 4.0 inches in Hamburg (Sussex County), 2.7 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 2.4 inches in Hope (Warren County) and 1.5 inches in High Bridge (Hunterdon County), Bernards (Somerset County), and Greenwich (Warren County). ||The wintry mix was caused by a strong surge of relatively warmer and more moist air above a surface warm front associated with an intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes. A weaker low pressure  system formed on the warm frontal boundary early in the morning of the 9th near the Virginia and North Carolina border. This slowed the advance of milder air into the higher terrain locations.

A major winter storm dropped heavy snow across all of New Jersey except for Sussex County. The heaviest snow fell along and east of the Interstate 95 corridor.   Snowfall averaged around 3 inches in Sussex County, 6 to 12 inches across the rest of northwest New Jersey, the Raritan Basin and Cape May County and 12 to 24 inches (with some locally higher amounts) across the rest of central and southern New Jersey. The 23.2 inches of snow that fell at the Philadelphia International Airport was the second heaviest snowfall on record.||Snow began around 1 a.m. EST across extreme southern New Jersey, but took a long time to spread north. Snow began around 7 a.m. EST along the Interstate 195 corridor, around Noon EST in the Raritan Basin and during the afternoon in northwest New Jersey. The snow mixed with sleet at times during the day on the 19th in southeast New Jersey and changed to sleet for a while in Cape May County. The snow fell at its heaviest between 6 a.m. EST and 6 p.m. EST across the southern fourth of New Jersey, between 10 a.m. EST and 10 p.m. EST across the northern half of southern New Jersey and during the afternoon and early evening across the Raritan Basin and north of Interstate 195 in central New Jersey. The snow ended between 1 a.m. and 3 a.m. EST at both ends of the state, but lingered through 8 a.m. EST in the central third of the state. | |A train engineer was injured after his New Jersey Transit Train struck a transit bus that became stuck on the tracks in Pennsauken (Camden County). All riders on the bus were evacuated before that occurred. New Jersey Transit suspended bus service during the evening of the 19th.  In Salem County, in Upper Pittsgrove, a motorist was injured after his vehicle flipped over. New Jersey River Line Transit Service was suspended around Camden into the 21st. In Monmouth County, the weight of the snow caused the collapse of a sports center bubble. The weight of the snow also caused the collapse of a 35 foot awning at a home in Toms River (Ocean County).|The winter storm postponed many planned local and high school sporting events. It came at an inopportune time for retailers as the Saturday (the 19th) before Christmas is usually one of the busiest shopping days of the year. Many malls in the southern half of New Jersey closed early. Many southern municipalities declared states of emergency. Since this storm occurred on the weekend, the number of traffic accidents was down. Nevertheless, there were 77 reported accidents in Burlington County and accidents snarled traffic on Interstates 295 and 676 in Cameden County.  AAA Mid Atlantic said that the volume of calls for a Saturday was 64 percent higher than normal; many were stranded motorists. Many school districts either closed schools or had two hour delayed openings on the 21st. Schools in Cumberland County remained closed through the planned start of the winter recess. Some churches cancelled services on the 20th. Trash pick-ups were also delayed. The winter storm also caused service interruptions on the 19th and 20th on the Cape May/Lewes Ferry.  | |Representative snowfall included 25.7 inches in Folsom (Atlantic County), 25.0 inches in Swedesboro (Gloucester County), 24.8 inches in Tabernacle (Burlington County), 24.0 inches in Atco (Camden County) and Newport (Cumberland County), 23.5 inches in Jackson (Ocean County) and Pittsgrove (Salem County), 22.5 inches in Colts Neck (Monmouth County), 22.0 inches in Hammonton (Atlantic County), 20.5 inches in Turnersville (Gloucester County), 19.5 inches in Cherry Hill (Camden County), 19.0 inches in Moorestown (Burlington County), 18.5 inches in Beachwood (Ocean County), 16.0 inches in Bridgeton (Cumberland County), 14.0 inches in Manalapan (Monmouth County), 12.1 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport, 12.0 inches in Princeton (Mercer County), 11.5 inches in Seaville (Cape May County), 10.3 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County), 10.0 inches in North Brunswick (Middlesex County), 9.5 inches in Hillsborough (Somerset County), 7.6 inches in Stewartsville (Warren County), 6.5 inches in Parsippany (Morris County) and Hackettstown (Warren County), 5.0 inches in West Cape May (Cape May County) and 3.9 inches in Sparta (Sussex County). ||The nor'easter responsible for the winter storm formed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 17th. It moved northeast along the northern Gulf of Mexico and was just south of Mobile, Alabama at sunrise on the 18th. From there it moved northeast and continued to intensify and reached Savannah, Georgia around 7 p.m. EST on the 18th, near Cape Hatteras, North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 19th. It continued to move northeast on the 19th and into the 20th and by sunrise on the 20th was located about 400 miles east of Seaside Heights, New Jersey.

In addition to the heavy snow, the nor'easter produced strong gusty northeast winds along the New Jersey coast from the morning into the evening of the 19th. This onshore flow also produced minor tidal flooding during the morning high tide on the 19th in Atlantic and Cape May Counties. Isolated minor tidal flooding also occurred with the morning high tide on the 20th in Atlantic County. About 3,000 homes and businesses lost power in Cape May County with Ocean City hit the hardest. The 9th Street Bridge was closed until the 21st in Ocean City because a tugboat broke free from its moorings and crashed against the bridge. Service was interrupted on the Cape May/Lewes Ferry on the 19th and 20th. The morning high tide on the 19th forced the closure of three bridges in Cape May County: The George Redding Bridge into Wildwood, The Townsend's Inlet Bridge between Avalon and Sea Isle City and the Beesleys Point Bridge between Upper Township and Somers Point. ||Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and included 52 mph at Barnegat Light (Ocean County), 49 mph at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County), 45 mph at Seaside Heights (Ocean County), 43 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County) and 40 mph in Cape May (Cape May County).||The high tide on the morning of the 19th reached 7.45 feet above mean lower low water at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water) and 6.25 feet above mean lower low water in Atlantic City (minor tidal flooding starts at 6.00 feet above mean lower low water).||The strong winds and tidal flooding were caused by an intense nor'easter that moved from the northern Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 18th northeast to around Cape Hatteras, North Carolina at sunrise on the 19th and then about 400 miles east of Seaside Heights, New Jersey at sunrise on the 20th. The strongest onshore flow occurred on the 19th and then slowly decreased that evening and overnight as the low pressure system moved farther offshore.

The morning high tide on the 19th forced the closure of three bridges in Cape May County: The George Redding Bridge into Wildwood, The Townsend's Inlet Bridge between Avalon and Sea Isle City and the Beesleys Point Bridge between Upper Township and Somers Point. ||Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and included 52 mph at Barnegat Light (Ocean County), 49 mph at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County), 45 mph at Seaside Heights (Ocean County), 43 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County) and 40 mph in Cape May (Cape May County).||The high tide on the morning of the 19th reached 7.45 feet above mean lower low water at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water) and 6.25 feet above mean lower low water in Atlantic City (minor tidal flooding starts at 6.00 feet above mean lower low water).||The strong winds and tidal flooding were caused by an intense nor'easter that moved from the northern Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 18th northeast to around Cape Hatteras, North Carolina at sunrise on the 19th and then about 400 miles east of Seaside Heights, New Jersey at sunrise on the 20th. The strongest onshore flow occurred on the 19th and then slowly decreased that evening and overnight as the low pressure system moved farther offshore.

Freezing rain occurred across most of the Raritan Basin as well as all of northwest New Jersey from the morning into the afternoon of the 13th. Ice accretions averaged between one and three tenths and led to many accidents on untreated roadways, bridges and overpasses. Some sleet also fell in Sussex County. Freezing rain began between 9 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST. As warmer air moved in at the surface, the freezing rain changed to rain early in the afternoon in Hunterdon and Somerset Counties, but took most of the rest of the afternoon for it to occur elsewhere in northwest New Jersey. Precipitation ended around 6 p.m. EST.  ||Numerous accidents were reported on Interstate 80 in Morris and Warren Counties and along Interstate 78, Interstate 287 and New Jersey State Route 23 in Morris County. Between 10 a.m. EST and 2 p.m. EST, New Jersey State Police alone reported 200 accidents. There were overturned vehicles and vehicles in ditches. Five injuries were reported.  Parts of Interstate 80 were closed in Warren County. In all about fourteen miles of Interstate 80 were closed.|  |The freezing rain resulted from precipitation that formed ahead of an advancing surface warm front. While it was warm enough aloft for rain, surface temperatures lagged.

The combination of melting snow and between 1.5 and 3.0 inches of rain produced field, poor drainage and widespread river flooding in New Jersey. Rain spread from south to north during the evening of the 25th and fell at a fairly steady rate. The heaviest rain fell during the afternoon of the 26th in the southern half of the state and the evening of the 26th in the northern half of the state. The rain ended during the evening of the 26th in the southern part of the state and the early morning of the 27th in the northern part of the state. ||The steady rain was caused by a strong southerly flow of air from the Gulf of Mexico around an intense low pressure system that spent much of the 25th and 26th in Iowa before weakening and drifting to the northeast. The mild southerly flow also quickly melted the large snow pack in the central and southern part of the state. The rain ended after this low's occluded front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain and snow melt started flooding on the Great Egg Harbor River on the 27th. The Great Egg Harbor River at Folsom was above its estimated 6 foot flood stage from 1120 a.m EST on the 27th through 9 p.m. EST on the 29th. It crested at 6.75 feet at 1200 p.m. EST on the 28th. Event precipitation totals included 2.71 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport, 2.51 inches in Hammonton, 2.42 inches in Folsom, 2.37 inches in Buena Vista and 2.15 inches in Estell Manor.

A weak low pressure system dropped light snow across northern and southwestern New Jersey and a wintry mix across southeast New Jersey during the morning of 31st. Snowfall averaged 1 to 3 inches with ice accretions less than one tenth of an inch. Precipitation began as a wintry mix across southeast New Jersey and as snow across southwest New Jersey between 4 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST. The snow spread north through the rest of New Jersey by 7 a.m. EST. The wintry mix changed to plain rain around the middle of the morning in southeast New Jersey and continued to fall as light snow elsewhere. All precipitation ended by Noon EST.  The precipitation's timing coupled with high traffic volume resulted in numerous accidents. The majority of the reported accidents were in southeastern New Jersey where the frozen precipitation fell primarily as freezing rain.||The most serious accident occurred in Upper Township of Cape May County when a 39-year-old mother and 8-year-old daughter died after she lost control of her vehicle on a curve and collided with a propane truck. In Atlantic County, there were two dozen reported accidents on the Atlantic City Expressway that resulted in five injuries. The tunnel of the Atlantic City Expressway Connector was closed because of an accident. The County Route 152 Bridge between Longport and Somers Point was closed. In Burlington County, the State Police reported about 30 accidents on the Garden State Parkway. In Cumberland County, over three dozen accidents were reported including State Route 47. In Mercer County, 10 accidents were reported in Princeton including an injury when a 36-year-old woman's Sports Utility Vehicle rolled over. ||Specific snowfall included 2.4 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.0 inches in Parsippany (Morris County) and Blairstown (Warren County), 1.8 inches in Whitehouse Station (Hunterdon County) and Branchburg (Somerset County), 1.4 inches in Lindenwold (Camden County), 1.3 inches in Florence (Burlington County), 1.2 inches in Metuchen (Middlesex County) and Cream Ridge (Morris County), 1.1 inches in Ewing (Mercer County) and 0.2 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport. Ice accretions were one tenth of an inch or less.||The low pressure system that caused the wintry mix moved northeast from Mobile, Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the 31st and reached Wilmington, North Carolina at 1 p.m. EST and was southeast of Long Island at 7 p.m. EST that evening.

Freezing rain occurred across most of the Raritan Basin as well as all of northwest New Jersey from the morning into the afternoon of the 13th. Ice accretions averaged between one and three tenths and led to many accidents on untreated roadways, bridges and overpasses. Some sleet also fell in Sussex County. Freezing rain began between 9 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST. As warmer air moved in at the surface, the freezing rain changed to rain early in the afternoon in Hunterdon and Somerset Counties, but took most of the rest of the afternoon for it to occur elsewhere in northwest New Jersey. Precipitation ended around 6 p.m. EST.  ||Numerous accidents were reported on Interstate 80 in Morris and Warren Counties and along Interstate 78, Interstate 287 and New Jersey State Route 23 in Morris County. Between 10 a.m. EST and 2 p.m. EST, New Jersey State Police alone reported 200 accidents. There were overturned vehicles and vehicles in ditches. Five injuries were reported.  Parts of Interstate 80 were closed in Warren County. In all about fourteen miles of Interstate 80 were closed.|  |The freezing rain resulted from precipitation that formed ahead of an advancing surface warm front. While it was warm enough aloft for rain, surface temperatures lagged.

A major winter storm dropped heavy snow across all of New Jersey except for Sussex County. The heaviest snow fell along and east of the Interstate 95 corridor.   Snowfall averaged around 3 inches in Sussex County, 6 to 12 inches across the rest of northwest New Jersey, the Raritan Basin and Cape May County and 12 to 24 inches (with some locally higher amounts) across the rest of central and southern New Jersey. The 23.2 inches of snow that fell at the Philadelphia International Airport was the second heaviest snowfall on record.||Snow began around 1 a.m. EST across extreme southern New Jersey, but took a long time to spread north. Snow began around 7 a.m. EST along the Interstate 195 corridor, around Noon EST in the Raritan Basin and during the afternoon in northwest New Jersey. The snow mixed with sleet at times during the day on the 19th in southeast New Jersey and changed to sleet for a while in Cape May County. The snow fell at its heaviest between 6 a.m. EST and 6 p.m. EST across the southern fourth of New Jersey, between 10 a.m. EST and 10 p.m. EST across the northern half of southern New Jersey and during the afternoon and early evening across the Raritan Basin and north of Interstate 195 in central New Jersey. The snow ended between 1 a.m. and 3 a.m. EST at both ends of the state, but lingered through 8 a.m. EST in the central third of the state. | |A train engineer was injured after his New Jersey Transit Train struck a transit bus that became stuck on the tracks in Pennsauken (Camden County). All riders on the bus were evacuated before that occurred. New Jersey Transit suspended bus service during the evening of the 19th.  In Salem County, in Upper Pittsgrove, a motorist was injured after his vehicle flipped over. New Jersey River Line Transit Service was suspended around Camden into the 21st. In Monmouth County, the weight of the snow caused the collapse of a sports center bubble. The weight of the snow also caused the collapse of a 35 foot awning at a home in Toms River (Ocean County).|The winter storm postponed many planned local and high school sporting events. It came at an inopportune time for retailers as the Saturday (the 19th) before Christmas is usually one of the busiest shopping days of the year. Many malls in the southern half of New Jersey closed early. Many southern municipalities declared states of emergency. Since this storm occurred on the weekend, the number of traffic accidents was down. Nevertheless, there were 77 reported accidents in Burlington County and accidents snarled traffic on Interstates 295 and 676 in Cameden County.  AAA Mid Atlantic said that the volume of calls for a Saturday was 64 percent higher than normal; many were stranded motorists. Many school districts either closed schools or had two hour delayed openings on the 21st. Schools in Cumberland County remained closed through the planned start of the winter recess. Some churches cancelled services on the 20th. Trash pick-ups were also delayed. The winter storm also caused service interruptions on the 19th and 20th on the Cape May/Lewes Ferry.  | |Representative snowfall included 25.7 inches in Folsom (Atlantic County), 25.0 inches in Swedesboro (Gloucester County), 24.8 inches in Tabernacle (Burlington County), 24.0 inches in Atco (Camden County) and Newport (Cumberland County), 23.5 inches in Jackson (Ocean County) and Pittsgrove (Salem County), 22.5 inches in Colts Neck (Monmouth County), 22.0 inches in Hammonton (Atlantic County), 20.5 inches in Turnersville (Gloucester County), 19.5 inches in Cherry Hill (Camden County), 19.0 inches in Moorestown (Burlington County), 18.5 inches in Beachwood (Ocean County), 16.0 inches in Bridgeton (Cumberland County), 14.0 inches in Manalapan (Monmouth County), 12.1 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport, 12.0 inches in Princeton (Mercer County), 11.5 inches in Seaville (Cape May County), 10.3 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County), 10.0 inches in North Brunswick (Middlesex County), 9.5 inches in Hillsborough (Somerset County), 7.6 inches in Stewartsville (Warren County), 6.5 inches in Parsippany (Morris County) and Hackettstown (Warren County), 5.0 inches in West Cape May (Cape May County) and 3.9 inches in Sparta (Sussex County). ||The nor'easter responsible for the winter storm formed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 17th. It moved northeast along the northern Gulf of Mexico and was just south of Mobile, Alabama at sunrise on the 18th. From there it moved northeast and continued to intensify and reached Savannah, Georgia around 7 p.m. EST on the 18th, near Cape Hatteras, North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 19th. It continued to move northeast on the 19th and into the 20th and by sunrise on the 20th was located about 400 miles east of Seaside Heights, New Jersey.

An intense low pressure system brought a wintry mix of snow and sleet to northwest New Jersey during the early morning of the 9th before the precipitation changed over to rain. Accumulations averaged 2 to 7 inches with the highest amounts over the higher terrain of Hunterdon, Morris, Sussex and Warren Counties. ||Snow began during the late evening of the 8th as far south as Somerset and Hunterdon Counties. As warmer air moved north, the snow changed to rain by around 3 a.m. EST in those counties. Farther to the north, snow continued to fall and changed to rain as late as around 6 a.m. EST in Sussex County. During the transition from snow to rain, some sleet also fell. The rain ended by Noon EST on the 9th. Most schools in northwest New Jersey had two hour delayed openings. ||Specific snowfall included 7.5 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 7.0 inches in Sussex (Sussex County), 6.1 inches in Jefferson (Morris County), 5.6 inches in Marcella and Rockaway (Morris County), 5.2 inches in Hardyston (Sussex County), 5.1 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 5.0 inches in Lebanon and Tewksbury (Hunterdon County) and Knowlton (Warren County), 4.8 inches in Roxbury (Morris County), 4.3 inches in Denville (Morris County), 4.0 inches in Hamburg (Sussex County), 2.7 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 2.4 inches in Hope (Warren County) and 1.5 inches in High Bridge (Hunterdon County), Bernards (Somerset County), and Greenwich (Warren County). ||The wintry mix was caused by a strong surge of relatively warmer and more moist air above a surface warm front associated with an intense low pressure system that moved through the Great Lakes. A weaker low pressure  system formed on the warm frontal boundary early in the morning of the 9th near the Virginia and North Carolina border. This slowed the advance of milder air into the higher terrain locations.
A strong pressure gradient between strong low pressure to the north and high pressure building from the west resulted in strong northwest winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds pushed over a tree in Paterson. The tree fell onto a car, a utility pole and a house on East 26th Street at 830 am. Extensive damage to a car occurred, and power was cut to the home. The home suffered minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure slowly tracked east across the United States and Canadian border from December 13th 2009 through December 14th 2009, with its trailing cold front slowly pushing through the region. Well below freezing temperatures in the preceding 48 hours and temperatures just below freezing during the onset of precipitation created a 2 to 4 hour period of light freezing rain that resulted in severe road icing across the region Sunday morning into early Sunday afternoon. Up to a tenth of an inch of freezing rain was reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple car accidents eastbound near exit 63.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty west winds followed a cold front passage across the New Jersey from the afternoon of the 19th into the afternoon of the 20th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph.||In Eatontown (Monmouth County), the strong winds contributed to the collapse of the vacant Eatontown Roller Rink on New Jersey State 35 and Clinton Avenue on the night of the 19th. The roof and sidewalls of the building partially collapsed. The building was scheduled to be demolished. No injuries were reported.||Peak wind gusts included 50 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 44 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport, 43 mph in Bivalve (Cumberland County) and 42 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County). The gusty winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that went slowly northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system that approached from the Mississippi Valley.

An area of high pressure early on the 10th gave way to an advancing low pressure system which traveled up the Ohio Valley. This system moved across the region during the nighttime hours of the 10th and then tracked to the northeast during the morning of the 11th. Moisture in the form of snow and rain overspread the area from southwest to northeast during the day on the 10th. Some of the snow was moderate to heavy for a time across the northern regions. There was actually a break in the precipitation for a time during the day on the 10th across central and into southern New Jersey. As milder air worked its way into the area, the snow and rain mixed with sleet and then changed over to some freezing rain and plain rain. The precipitation ended from west to east during the morning hours of the 11th.||Minor accidents were reported on Interstate 78 in Warren County on the 10th due to snow that produced slippery conditions. Many local roads were also slippery which resulted in car accidents, some of which involved minor injuries. Snow caused slippery roadways in Morris County on the 10th. The State Police reported that conditions on the highways were slippery during the afternoon, particularly on the ramps. During the evening, it was reported that the conditions worsened quite a bit, especially on Route 80. Jersey Central Power and Light reported a few outages in Morris County. The snowy conditions prompted cancellations of several community events or some changed their scheduled times. Jersey Central Power and Light reported a few outages in Sussex County on the 10th. There was just enough icing that produced slippery conditions in other areas, particularly on bridges, overpasses and other elevated structures.||Some snowfall totals included; 3.3 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 3.1 inches in Oak Ridge (Morris County), 3.0 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.8 inches in Califon (Hunterdon County), 2.8 inches in Hopatcong (Sussex County), 2.3 inches in Butler (Morris County), 2.1 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County), 2.0 inches in Metuchen (Middlesex County), 2.0 inches in Whitehouse (Hunterdon County), 1.9 inches in Neshanic (Somerset County), 1.5 inches in Hillsborough (Somerset County), 1.3 inches in Edison (Middlesex County), 1.0 inch in Belle Mead (Somerset County), 1.0 inch in Old Bridge (Middlesex County), 0.8 of an inch in Holmdel (Monmouth County), 0.7 of an inch in Ewing (Mercer County), 0.5 of an inch in Colts Neck (Monmouth County), 0.2 of an inch in Edgewater Park (Burlington County), 0.1 of an inch in Mount Holly (Burlington County) and 0.1 of an inch in National Park (Gloucester County). Ice accumulations were generally less than one tenth of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few periods of snow which mixed with rain and sleet and even some freezing rain moved in during the early evening hours of the 10th. While there were some breaks in the precipitation, temperatures were right around freezing which resulted in some icy spots.

There was just enough icing that produced slippery conditions in other areas, particularly on bridges, overpasses and other elevated structures.||Some snowfall totals included; 3.3 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 3.1 inches in Oak Ridge (Morris County), 3.0 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.8 inches in Califon (Hunterdon County), 2.8 inches in Hopatcong (Sussex County), 2.3 inches in Butler (Morris County), 2.1 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County), 2.0 inches in Metuchen (Middlesex County), 2.0 inches in Whitehouse (Hunterdon County), 1.9 inches in Neshanic (Somerset County), 1.5 inches in Hillsborough (Somerset County), 1.3 inches in Edison (Middlesex County), 1.0 inch in Belle Mead (Somerset County), 1.0 inch in Old Bridge (Middlesex County), 0.8 of an inch in Holmdel (Monmouth County), 0.7 of an inch in Ewing (Mercer County), 0.5 of an inch in Colts Neck (Monmouth County), 0.2 of an inch in Edgewater Park (Burlington County), 0.1 of an inch in Mount Holly (Burlington County) and 0.1 of an inch in National Park (Gloucester County). Ice accumulations were generally less than one tenth of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few periods of snow which mixed with rain and sleet and even some freezing rain moved in during the early evening hours of the 10th. While there were some breaks in the precipitation, temperatures were right around freezing which resulted in some icy spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent pair of storms moved through the Mid Atlantic and Northeast during the 7th and into the 8th. One potent storm moved across the eastern Great Lakes region during the evening of the 7th, while a secondary potent storm developed along a front off the New Jersey coast. Both of these storms combined into one large and intense storm early on the 8th across the Canadian Maritimes. Behind these storms, much colder air flowed across the region, and this combined with a tightened pressure gradient, which produced strong offshore winds. This caused blow-out tides to occur during the late evening hours of the 8th.||The water level at the Sandy Hook tidal gage dropped to 1.81 feet below mean lower low water at 10:48 PM EST on the 8th then bottomed out at 1.88 feet below mean lower low water at 11:18 PM EST on the 8th. The water level at the Barnegat Light tidal gage dropped to 1.80 feet below mean lower low water at 10:54 PM EST on the 8th then bottomed out at 1.86 feet below mean lower low water at 11:18 PM EST on the 8th. The water level at the Atlantic City tidal gage dropped to 1.81 feet below mean lower low water at 9:36 PM EST on the 8th and bottomed out at 2.17 feet below mean lower low water between 10:12 PM EST on the 8th. Farther to the south, the water level at the Cape May tidal gage dropped to 1.86 feet below lower low water at 11:06 PM EST on the 8th then bottomed out at 2.13 feet below lower low water between 11:48 PM EST and 11:54 PM EST on the 8th. Blow-out tides begin at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

An area of low pressure moved through the Great Lakes region during the 17th and 18th. This system was more like an Alberta Clipper as it originated near the providence of Alberta Canada. This system spread a swath of snow across northern into central New Jersey during the late evening hours of the 17th, although the bulk of the accumulating snow occurred across the far northwestern part of the state. The snow then tapered off and ended during the mid to late morning hours of the 18th.||Some snowfall totals included; 3.5 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.8 inches in Lake Hopatcong (Morris County), 2.6 inches in Columbia (Warren County), 2.5 inches in Newton (Sussex County), 2.5 inches in Allomuchy (Warren County), 2.4 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 2.0 inches in Rockaway (Morris County), 2.0 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County), 1.8 inches in Butler (Morris County), 0.9 of an inch in Somerville (Somerset County), 0.8 of an inch in Whitehouse (Hunterdon County), 0.6 of an inch in Hillsborough (Somerset County) and 0.1 of an inch in Mount Holly (Burlington County).

An area of high pressure early on the 10th gave way to an advancing low pressure system which traveled up the Ohio Valley. This system moved across the region during the nighttime hours of the 10th and then tracked to the northeast during the morning of the 11th. Moisture in the form of snow and rain overspread the area from southwest to northeast during the day on the 10th. Some of the snow was moderate to heavy for a time across the northern regions. There was actually a break in the precipitation for a time during the day on the 10th across central and into southern New Jersey. As milder air worked its way into the area, the snow and rain mixed with sleet and then changed over to some freezing rain and plain rain. The precipitation ended from west to east during the morning hours of the 11th.||Minor accidents were reported on Interstate 78 in Warren County on the 10th due to snow that produced slippery conditions. A potent pair of storms moved through the Mid Atlantic and Northeast during the 7th and into the 8th. One potent storm moved across the eastern Great Lakes region during the evening of the 7th, while a secondary potent storm developed along a front off the New Jersey coast. Both of these storms combined into one large and intense storm early on the 8th across the Canadian Maritimes. Blow-out tides begin at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

One potent storm moved across the eastern Great Lakes region during the evening of the 7th, while a secondary potent storm developed along a front off the New Jersey coast. Both of these storms combined into one large and intense storm early on the 8th across the Canadian Maritimes. Behind these storms, much colder air flowed across the region, and this combined with a tightened pressure gradient, which produced strong offshore winds. This caused blow-out tides to occur during the late evening hours of the 8th.||The water level at the Sandy Hook tidal gage dropped to 1.81 feet below mean lower low water at 10:48 PM EST on the 8th then bottomed out at 1.88 feet below mean lower low water at 11:18 PM EST on the 8th. The water level at the Barnegat Light tidal gage dropped to 1.80 feet below mean lower low water at 10:54 PM EST on the 8th then bottomed out at 1.86 feet below mean lower low water at 11:18 PM EST on the 8th. The water level at the Atlantic City tidal gage dropped to 1.81 feet below mean lower low water at 9:36 PM EST on the 8th and bottomed out at 2.17 feet below mean lower low water between 10:12 PM EST on the 8th. Farther to the south, the water level at the Cape May tidal gage dropped to 1.86 feet below lower low water at 11:06 PM EST on the 8th then bottomed out at 2.13 feet below lower low water between 11:48 PM EST and 11:54 PM EST on the 8th. Blow-out tides begin at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

A frontal boundary was draped from the southern Mid Atlantic region southwestward to the northern Gulf of Mexico. An area of low pressure developed along this front across the lower Mississippi Valley late in the day on the 27th. The storm system then moved northeastward and tracked near the Ohio River Valley during the morning of the 28th. As the storm continued to track northeastward, it strengthened as it moved into New England during the early evening hours of the 28th. An associated cold front then swept across the Mid Atlantic region during the early evening hours of the 28th.||A cold air mass ahead of the system allowed for snow to overspread the state generally from south to north during the 27th. As milder air then worked in especially above the surface, the snow changed to sleet and freezing rain during the night of the 27th mainly across the southern and central parts of the state. Enough warming occurred during the 28th, which allowed the wintry mix to change over to plain rain before ending mainly across the central and southern parts of the state. The mixed precipitation held on the longest across the northwestern part of the state as the cold air remained entrenched longer. The combination of the frozen and freezing precipitation along with the cold temperatures, created a high impact event for most of the state. This also produced very slippery roadways across the state.||The Hunterdon Medical Center in Raritan Township (Hunterdon County) was briefly on backup generators on the 28th due to the winter storm, which caused some problems with power lines in the area. Nineteen weather-related wrecks were reported in Warren County through 12:00 PM EST on the 28th, which included a jack-knifed tractor-trailer on a back road in Mansfield Township. The mixture of snow, sleet and rain late on the 27th and on the 28th produced messy road conditions and prompted schools to close across Morris County. Roads were particularly bad during the morning of the 28th as the snow fell at nearly an inch per hour for a time. Around 12:00 PM EST on the 28th, two lanes of Route 287 northbound were closed after a truck flipped near Exit 32 in Harding (Morris County). At about the same time, an entrance ramp on Route 80 in Mount Olive was closed because of an overturned vehicle. Less than a dozen accidents were reported in Washington Township. During the afternoon of the 28th, flooding became a problem in some areas as slush clogged storm drains and created pockets of water at intersections. An acceleration lane on Route 80 eastbound in Denville was closed briefly at 2:00 PM EST on the 28th after a large area of standing water formed. Flooding also caused some problems in Parsippany at both Baldwin and Parsippany roads. Some power outages were reported however these were mostly minimal in scope according to officials.||The winter storm produced a couple inches of snow late on the 27th and the 28th, however as milder air moved in, dense fog overspread the Ocean County area. The visibility was reduced to near zero in many areas. During the evening hours of the 28th, a fog warning was issued for the entire length of the Garden State Parkway. Due to the inclement weather, as many as 500 Jersey Central Power and Light customers lost power in the Barnegat Light area. Also, most public schools altered their schedules due to the wintry weather.||An 8-year-old girl in Woodbury (Gloucester County) was injured during the early afternoon hours of the 28th when the sled she was on collided with an SUV. The girl was sledding down a driveway and entered the street then went under the SUV, which was pulling away from the curb. The girl had to be extricated from the vehicle. In Woolwich Township (Gloucester County), the southbound lanes of the New Jersey Turnpike were blocked for about three hours during the early morning hours of the 28th after a car and a tractor-trailer collided. A female in the car was taken to a nearby hospital for serious head injuries. The State Police reported that the accident was weather related. The winter storm produced hazardous road conditions late on the 27th and on the 28th in Sussex County.
