A large arctic high pressure system moved toward the area during the 16th and 17th. The extent of the arctic air mass kept maximum temperatures only in the teens, with even some single digits across northwestern New Jersey. The minimum temperatures dropped into the single digits, however some areas where the wind was able to drop off considerably, mainly in the northern part of the state, dropped to well below zero. As a storm system remained well to the northeast, the combination of this along with strong arctic high pressure to the west allowed for gusty winds. This produced wind chill values to zero and below zero for awhile, especially during the nighttime hours. The coldest wind chill value was recorded at the Sussex Airport (Sussex County) of 14 degrees below zero at 7:00 AM EST on the 17th. The majority of the state experienced wind chill values between 0 and 10 degrees below zero during early morning hours of the 16th and 17th.||The blast of arctic air from about the 14th through the 17th made the Trenton Rescue Mission brace for an influx of clients at the facility on Carroll Street. The Mission planned some action with their sister nonprofits and the city of Trenton. The Mercer County Executive���s Office issued a weather alert to residents because of the cold snap expected. One of the shelters in West Windsor was opened on the 15th due to the arctic cold. The low temperatures on the 17th were the coldest in awhile across the region. As an example, 4 degrees was the morning low at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona (Atlantic County), which was the coldest since March 8, 2007 when it was 4 degrees. In addition, it was 4 degrees on the 17th at the Trenton Mercer County Airport (Mercer County), which was the coldest since January 29, 2005 when it was 4 degrees.||Some low temperatures on the 16th included; 15 degrees below zero at the Sussex Airport (Sussex County), 12 degrees below zero in Walpack (Sussex County), 8 degrees below zero in Pequest (Warren County), 7 degrees below zero in Hope (Warren County), 2 degrees below zero in Basking Ridge (Somerset County), 1 degree below zero in Wantage (Sussex County), 6 degrees below zero at Andover (Sussex County), 2 degrees in Netcong (Morris County), 7 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County), 12 degrees in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 12 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona (Atlantic County), and 16 degrees at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County).||Some low temperatures on the 17th included; 15 degrees below zero in Sussex (Sussex County), 12 degrees below zero in Walpack (Sussex County), 10 degrees below zero in Hope (Warren County), 8 degrees below zero in Andover (Sussex County), 4 degrees below zero in Oswego Lake (Burlington County), 2 degrees below zero in Millville (Cumberland County), 2 degrees below zero in Somerville (Somerset County), 0 in Red Lion (Middlesex County), 3 degrees in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 4 degrees at the Trenton Airport (Mercer County), 4 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona (Atlantic County) and 10 degrees at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County).

An area of high pressure early on the 10th gave way to an advancing low pressure system which traveled up the Ohio Valley. This system moved across the region during the nighttime hours of the 10th and then tracked to the northeast during the morning of the 11th. Moisture in the form of snow and rain overspread the area from southwest to northeast during the day on the 10th. Some of the snow was moderate to heavy for a time across the northern regions. There was actually a break in the precipitation for a time during the day on the 10th across central and into southern New Jersey. As milder air worked its way into the area, the snow and rain mixed with sleet and then changed over to some freezing rain and plain rain. The precipitation ended from west to east during the morning hours of the 11th.||Minor accidents were reported on Interstate 78 in Warren County on the 10th due to snow that produced slippery conditions. Many local roads were also slippery which resulted in car accidents, some of which involved minor injuries. Snow caused slippery roadways in Morris County on the 10th. The State Police reported that conditions on the highways were slippery during the afternoon, particularly on the ramps. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the Gulf Coasts states during the 27th. A warm front extended northeastward from this storm into the southern Mid Atlantic region. Meanwhile, a cold dome of air was entrenched over the area while milder air gradually moved northward in association with the warm front. As the milder and even more moist air arrived over the snow on the ground, areas of dense fog developed across the southern portions of the state during the early morning hours of the 28th, then spread north and westward during the day. The local visibilities lowered to under one-quarter of a mile at times at some locations during the height of the dense fog. A cold front moved through during the early evening of the 28th, which quickly dissipated the areas of dense fog.||While most reporting stations indicated the visibility lowered to one-quarter of a mile, the Millville Airport experienced a visibility down to zero between 7:00 AM EST and 11:00 AM EST on the 28th. Some spotter reports as well as traffic cameras indicated that the visibility in some other areas were near zero at times. The dense fog in combination with wet to slushy roads created tricky travel as the visibility was greatly reduced for awhile on the 28th. The dense fog impacted the area bridges, and the speed limit on the Ben Franklin Bridge was lowered to 35 mph because of reduced visibilities.

An area of low pressure developed across the Gulf Coasts states during the 27th. A warm front extended northeastward from this storm into the southern Mid Atlantic region. Meanwhile, a cold dome of air was entrenched over the area while milder air gradually moved northward in association with the warm front. As the milder and even more moist air arrived over the snow on the ground, areas of dense fog developed across the southern portions of the state during the early morning hours of the 28th, then spread north and westward during the day. The local visibilities lowered to under one-quarter of a mile at times at some locations during the height of the dense fog. A cold front moved through during the early evening of the 28th, which quickly dissipated the areas of dense fog.||While most reporting stations indicated the visibility lowered to one-quarter of a mile, the Millville Airport experienced a visibility down to zero between 7:00 AM EST and 11:00 AM EST on the 28th. Some spotter reports as well as traffic cameras indicated that the visibility in some other areas were near zero at times. The dense fog in combination with wet to slushy roads created tricky travel as the visibility was greatly reduced for awhile on the 28th. The dense fog impacted the area bridges, and the speed limit on the Ben Franklin Bridge was lowered to 35 mph because of reduced visibilities.

A large arctic high pressure system moved toward the area during the 16th and 17th. The extent of the arctic air mass kept maximum temperatures only in the teens, with even some single digits across northwestern New Jersey. The minimum temperatures dropped into the single digits, however some areas where the wind was able to drop off considerably, mainly in the northern part of the state, dropped to well below zero. As a storm system remained well to the northeast, the combination of this along with strong arctic high pressure to the west allowed for gusty winds. This produced wind chill values to zero and below zero for awhile, especially during the nighttime hours. The coldest wind chill value was recorded at the Sussex Airport (Sussex County) of 14 degrees below zero at 7:00 AM EST on the 17th. The majority of the state experienced wind chill values between 0 and 10 degrees below zero during early morning hours of the 16th and 17th.||The blast of arctic air from about the 14th through the 17th made the Trenton Rescue Mission brace for an influx of clients at the facility on Carroll Street. The Mission planned some action with their sister nonprofits and the city of Trenton. The Mercer County Executive���s Office issued a weather alert to residents because of the cold snap expected. One of the shelters in West Windsor was opened on the 15th due to the arctic cold. The low temperatures on the 17th were the coldest in awhile across the region. As an example, 4 degrees was the morning low at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona (Atlantic County), which was the coldest since March 8, 2007 when it was 4 degrees. In addition, it was 4 degrees on the 17th at the Trenton Mercer County Airport (Mercer County), which was the coldest since January 29, 2005 when it was 4 degrees.||Some low temperatures on the 16th included; 15 degrees below zero at the Sussex Airport (Sussex County), 12 degrees below zero in Walpack (Sussex County), 8 degrees below zero in Pequest (Warren County), 7 degrees below zero in Hope (Warren County), 2 degrees below zero in Basking Ridge (Somerset County), 1 degree below zero in Wantage (Sussex County), 6 degrees below zero at Andover (Sussex County), 2 degrees in Netcong (Morris County), 7 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County), 12 degrees in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 12 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona (Atlantic County), and 16 degrees at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County).||Some low temperatures on the 17th included; 15 degrees below zero in Sussex (Sussex County), 12 degrees below zero in Walpack (Sussex County), 10 degrees below zero in Hope (Warren County), 8 degrees below zero in Andover (Sussex County), 4 degrees below zero in Oswego Lake (Burlington County), 2 degrees below zero in Millville (Cumberland County), 2 degrees below zero in Somerville (Somerset County), 0 in Red Lion (Middlesex County), 3 degrees in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 4 degrees at the Trenton Airport (Mercer County), 4 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona (Atlantic County) and 10 degrees at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County).

An area of high pressure early on the 10th gave way to an advancing low pressure system which traveled up the Ohio Valley. This system moved across the region during the nighttime hours of the 10th and then tracked to the northeast during the morning of the 11th. Moisture in the form of snow and rain overspread the area from southwest to northeast during the day on the 10th. Some of the snow was moderate to heavy for a time across the northern regions. There was actually a break in the precipitation for a time during the day on the 10th across central and into southern New Jersey. As milder air worked its way into the area, the snow and rain mixed with sleet and then changed over to some freezing rain and plain rain. The precipitation ended from west to east during the morning hours of the 11th.||Minor accidents were reported on Interstate 78 in Warren County on the 10th due to snow that produced slippery conditions. Many local roads were also slippery which resulted in car accidents, some of which involved minor injuries. Snow caused slippery roadways in Morris County on the 10th. The State Police reported that conditions on the highways were slippery during the afternoon, particularly on the ramps. During the evening, it was reported that the conditions worsened quite a bit, especially on Route 80. Jersey Central Power and Light reported a few outages in Morris County. The snowy conditions prompted cancellations of several community events or some changed their scheduled times. Jersey Central Power and Light reported a few outages in Sussex County on the 10th. There was just enough icing that produced slippery conditions in other areas, particularly on bridges, overpasses and other elevated structures.||Some snowfall totals included; 3.3 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 3.1 inches in Oak Ridge (Morris County), 3.0 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.8 inches in Califon (Hunterdon County), 2.8 inches in Hopatcong (Sussex County), 2.3 inches in Butler (Morris County), 2.1 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County), 2.0 inches in Metuchen (Middlesex County), 2.0 inches in Whitehouse (Hunterdon County), 1.9 inches in Neshanic (Somerset County), 1.5 inches in Hillsborough (Somerset County), 1.3 inches in Edison (Middlesex County), 1.0 inch in Belle Mead (Somerset County), 1.0 inch in Old Bridge (Middlesex County), 0.8 of an inch in Holmdel (Monmouth County), 0.7 of an inch in Ewing (Mercer County), 0.5 of an inch in Colts Neck (Monmouth County), 0.2 of an inch in Edgewater Park (Burlington County), 0.1 of an inch in Mount Holly (Burlington County) and 0.1 of an inch in National Park (Gloucester County). Ice accumulations were generally less than one tenth of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few periods of snow which mixed with rain and sleet and even some freezing rain moved in during the early evening hours of the 10th. While there were some breaks in the precipitation, temperatures were right around freezing which resulted in some icy spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent pair of storms moved through the Mid Atlantic and Northeast during the 7th and into the 8th. One potent storm moved across the eastern Great Lakes region during the evening of the 7th, while a secondary potent storm developed along a front off the New Jersey coast. Both of these storms combined into one large and intense storm early on the 8th across the Canadian Maritimes. Behind these storms, much colder air flowed across the region, and this combined with a tightened pressure gradient, which produced strong offshore winds. This caused blow-out tides to occur during the late evening hours of the 8th.||The water level at the Sandy Hook tidal gage dropped to 1.81 feet below mean lower low water at 10:48 PM EST on the 8th then bottomed out at 1.88 feet below mean lower low water at 11:18 PM EST on the 8th. The water level at the Barnegat Light tidal gage dropped to 1.80 feet below mean lower low water at 10:54 PM EST on the 8th then bottomed out at 1.86 feet below mean lower low water at 11:18 PM EST on the 8th. The water level at the Atlantic City tidal gage dropped to 1.81 feet below mean lower low water at 9:36 PM EST on the 8th and bottomed out at 2.17 feet below mean lower low water between 10:12 PM EST on the 8th. Farther to the south, the water level at the Cape May tidal gage dropped to 1.86 feet below lower low water at 11:06 PM EST on the 8th then bottomed out at 2.13 feet below lower low water between 11:48 PM EST and 11:54 PM EST on the 8th. Blow-out tides begin at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

A weak clipper storm system moved across the area on the 15th, then strengthened some as it moved east of the New Jersey Coast. This threw additional moisture back into parts of New Jersey, which allowed 0.5 of an inch to 1.5 inches of snow to accumulate. The cold conditions combined with the snowfall and produced slippery conditions for a time on the 15th. This resulted in numerous accidents as roadways became slippery. There were no reports of injuries or fatalities. ||A snowy start to the day on the 15th took a toll on the roadways in Ocean County. In Toms River, police reported more than 40 minor motor vehicle accidents between 8:00 AM EST and 1:00 PM EST, and the majority of these were weather related. An accident on Interstate 287 northbound in Bridgewater Township (Somerset County), during wintry precipitation on the 15th closed all the lanes.||Some specific snow totals included; 2.3 inches in Edison (Middlesex County), 1.5 inches in Lawrenceville (Mercer County), 1.5 inches in Hillsborough (Somerset County), 1.3 inches in Somerset (Somerset County), 1.3 inches in Green Pond Junction (Morris County), 1.1 inches in Somerville (Somerset County), 0.9 of an inch in Stewartsville (Warren County), 0.9 of an inch in Ewing (Mercer County), 0.8 of an inch in Bayville (Ocean County), 0.4 of an inch in Mount Holly (Burlington County) and 0.2 of an inch in Mount Laurel (Burlington County).

A brush fire caused by a downed utility line damaged 150 acres of Troy Meadows wetlands from the night of the 18th into the morning of the 19th. The fire was reported shortly after 6 p.m. EST along the Parsippany and Hanover municipal border, across Troy Road from the Hanover Sewerage Authority. An east wind helped fuel the blaze through open fields in Parsippany and East Hanover. As the fire spread through dry grass and weeds and sent up a thick column of smoke, state firefighters and local fire departments worked through the night plowing dirt perimeters to contain the blaze. Houses about half a mile away from the blaze on South Beverwyck Road were not threatened. The fire was reported 100 percent contained at 9 a.m. EST on the 19th. The high humidity and showers helped get it under control at noon EST on the 19th. South Beverwyck and Troy Roads were closed due to the fire. No injuries were reported. One household lost power, and Jersey Central Power and Light stopped power to 50 other customers to restore the lines. Most power was restored within 15 minutes. Peak wind gusts at Newark International Airport on both the 18th and 19th were around 25 mph.

An area of low pressure moved through the Great Lakes region during the 17th and 18th. This system was more like an Alberta Clipper as it originated near the providence of Alberta Canada. This system spread a swath of snow across northern into central New Jersey during the late evening hours of the 17th, although the bulk of the accumulating snow occurred across the far northwestern part of the state. The snow then tapered off and ended during the mid to late morning hours of the 18th.||Some snowfall totals included; 3.5 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.8 inches in Lake Hopatcong (Morris County), 2.6 inches in Columbia (Warren County), 2.5 inches in Newton (Sussex County), 2.5 inches in Allomuchy (Warren County), 2.4 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 2.0 inches in Rockaway (Morris County), 2.0 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County), 1.8 inches in Butler (Morris County), 0.9 of an inch in Somerville (Somerset County), 0.8 of an inch in Whitehouse (Hunterdon County), 0.6 of an inch in Hillsborough (Somerset County) and 0.1 of an inch in Mount Holly (Burlington County).

An area of low pressure developed across the Gulf Coasts states during the 27th. A warm front extended northeastward from this storm into the southern Mid Atlantic region. Meanwhile, a cold dome of air was entrenched over the area while milder air gradually moved northward in association with the warm front. As the milder and even more moist air arrived over the snow on the ground, areas of dense fog developed across the southern portions of the state during the early morning hours of the 28th, then spread north and westward during the day. The local visibilities lowered to under one-quarter of a mile at times at some locations during the height of the dense fog. A cold front moved through during the early evening of the 28th, which quickly dissipated the areas of dense fog.||While most reporting stations indicated the visibility lowered to one-quarter of a mile, the Millville Airport experienced a visibility down to zero between 7:00 AM EST and 11:00 AM EST on the 28th. Some spotter reports as well as traffic cameras indicated that the visibility in some other areas were near zero at times. The dense fog in combination with wet to slushy roads created tricky travel as the visibility was greatly reduced for awhile on the 28th. The dense fog impacted the area bridges, and the speed limit on the Ben Franklin Bridge was lowered to 35 mph because of reduced visibilities.

Strong gusty west winds followed a cold front passage across the New Jersey from the afternoon of the 19th into the afternoon of the 20th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph.||In Eatontown (Monmouth County), the strong winds contributed to the collapse of the vacant Eatontown Roller Rink on New Jersey State 35 and Clinton Avenue on the night of the 19th. The roof and sidewalls of the building partially collapsed. The building was scheduled to be demolished. No injuries were reported.||Peak wind gusts included 50 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 44 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport, 43 mph in Bivalve (Cumberland County) and 42 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County). The gusty winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that went slowly northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system that approached from the Mississippi Valley.

A wind whipped forest fire burned about 40 acres in Galloway Township during the afternoon and evening on the 12th before it was contained. The fire was believed to be caused by arcing downed wires. The fire occurred in the area bounded by Leipzig Avenue and Liebig and Herschel Streets. No homes were damaged or threatened and no injuries were reported.

A large arctic high pressure system moved toward the area during the 16th and 17th. The extent of the arctic air mass kept maximum temperatures only in the teens, with even some single digits across northwestern New Jersey. The minimum temperatures dropped into the single digits, however some areas where the wind was able to drop off considerably, mainly in the northern part of the state, dropped to well below zero. As a storm system remained well to the northeast, the combination of this along with strong arctic high pressure to the west allowed for gusty winds. This produced wind chill values to zero and below zero for awhile, especially during the nighttime hours. The coldest wind chill value was recorded at the Sussex Airport (Sussex County) of 14 degrees below zero at 7:00 AM EST on the 17th. The majority of the state experienced wind chill values between 0 and 10 degrees below zero during early morning hours of the 16th and 17th.||The blast of arctic air from about the 14th through the 17th made the Trenton Rescue Mission brace for an influx of clients at the facility on Carroll Street. The Mission planned some action with their sister nonprofits and the city of Trenton. The Mercer County Executive���s Office issued a weather alert to residents because of the cold snap expected. One of the shelters in West Windsor was opened on the 15th due to the arctic cold. The low temperatures on the 17th were the coldest in awhile across the region. As an example, 4 degrees was the morning low at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona (Atlantic County), which was the coldest since March 8, 2007 when it was 4 degrees. In addition, it was 4 degrees on the 17th at the Trenton Mercer County Airport (Mercer County), which was the coldest since January 29, 2005 when it was 4 degrees.||Some low temperatures on the 16th included; 15 degrees below zero at the Sussex Airport (Sussex County), 12 degrees below zero in Walpack (Sussex County), 8 degrees below zero in Pequest (Warren County), 7 degrees below zero in Hope (Warren County), 2 degrees below zero in Basking Ridge (Somerset County), 1 degree below zero in Wantage (Sussex County), 6 degrees below zero at Andover (Sussex County), 2 degrees in Netcong (Morris County), 7 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County), 12 degrees in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 12 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona (Atlantic County), and 16 degrees at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County).||Some low temperatures on the 17th included; 15 degrees below zero in Sussex (Sussex County), 12 degrees below zero in Walpack (Sussex County), 10 degrees below zero in Hope (Warren County), 8 degrees below zero in Andover (Sussex County), 4 degrees below zero in Oswego Lake (Burlington County), 2 degrees below zero in Millville (Cumberland County), 2 degrees below zero in Somerville (Somerset County), 0 in Red Lion (Middlesex County), 3 degrees in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 4 degrees at the Trenton Airport (Mercer County), 4 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona (Atlantic County) and 10 degrees at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County).

Strong gusty west winds followed a cold front passage across the New Jersey from the afternoon of the 19th into the afternoon of the 20th. Gusty west to northwest winds affected higher terrain and coastal locations of New Jersey from the afternoon of the 22nd into the afternoon of the 23rd. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph. The strong winds began after a cold frontal passage during the afternoon of the 22nd. Most of the peak wind gusts occurred during the evening of the 22nd into the early morning of the 23rd. Winds did not diminish until the evening of the 23rd. ||The strong winds created havoc for fire fighters and emergency responders battling a blaze at the Environmental Protection Agency facility in Edison (Middlesex County) on the 23rd. The fire badly damaged two trailers at the complex. The wind tossed flaming debris throughout the complex. Two fire fighters were injured.||Peak wind gusts included 53 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 52 mph in High Point (Sussex County), 51 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and 50 mph in Cape May (Cape May County).||The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system as it moved from the Great Lakes and through the Canadian Maritimes and a strong high pressure system that was building east from the Upper Mississippi Valley. ||While there were a couple of wind events during the month of February, there was not much precipitation in New Jersey. It was the driest February on record at both the Atlantic City International Airport (0.68 inches) and Trenton (0.64 inches).  On a statewide average, it was also the driest February on record dating back to 1895. The statewide average precipitation of 0.64 inches was only about 20 percent of the normal February precipitation.

An area of low pressure developed across the Gulf Coasts states during the 27th. A warm front extended northeastward from this storm into the southern Mid Atlantic region. Meanwhile, a cold dome of air was entrenched over the area while milder air gradually moved northward in association with the warm front. As the milder and even more moist air arrived over the snow on the ground, areas of dense fog developed across the southern portions of the state during the early morning hours of the 28th, then spread north and westward during the day. The local visibilities lowered to under one-quarter of a mile at times at some locations during the height of the dense fog. A cold front moved through during the early evening of the 28th, which quickly dissipated the areas of dense fog.||While most reporting stations indicated the visibility lowered to one-quarter of a mile, the Millville Airport experienced a visibility down to zero between 7:00 AM EST and 11:00 AM EST on the 28th. Some spotter reports as well as traffic cameras indicated that the visibility in some other areas were near zero at times. The dense fog in combination with wet to slushy roads created tricky travel as the visibility was greatly reduced for awhile on the 28th. The dense fog impacted the area bridges, and the speed limit on the Ben Franklin Bridge was lowered to 35 mph because of reduced visibilities.

An area of high pressure moved across the region during the 6th. This allowed colder and much drier air to settle southward into the Middle Atlantic region. Meanwhile, an area of low pressure organized across the Tennessee Valley during the morning of the 6th and tracked northward to the northern Ohio Valley during the evening of the 6th. An abundance of moisture surged well ahead of this storm. Some areas experienced some sleet and snow to start as the precipitation intensity was rather light. As the precipitation intensified and increased in areal coverage, freezing rain, sleet, rain and even some snow occurred. However, freezing rain and rain were the dominant precipitation type. Significant icing occurred across northwestern New Jersey, with a coating of ice all the way down to portions of west-central and southwestern New Jersey. The main low pressure system then tracked to the north on the 7th, as a secondary low pressure center formed just south of Long Island. The two systems produced plenty of rain across the area, although freezing rain held on across a portion of northwestern New Jersey for nearly the entire duration. An associated cold front finally moved through the region during the evening of the 7th, which brought drier air into the area.||The most significant icing occurred across Warren, Hunterdon, Morris and especially Sussex counties. Icy conditions in Warren County contributed to an accident on Interstate 80 at Mile Marker 2. The accident, which occurred on a section of the ���S��� turns, slowed traffic for more than an hour during the morning of the 7th.||About 400 customers scattered throughout Morris County lost power by about 12:00 PM EST on the 7th, primarily due to ice-laden tree branches that fell onto electrical lines. In Randolph, the westbound lanes of Route 10 were closed at Dover-Chester Road in the morning of the 7th because a tree fell across the highway, which blocked all three westbound lanes. In Morris Township, Sussex Avenue was closed from Sudberry Drive to Bradwahl Drive because a tree fell due to the accumulated ice and brought down utility wires around 2:20 PM EST on the 7th. In Mount Olive, 10 weather-related vehicle accidents were reported between 5:00 PM EST on the 7th and 7:00 AM EST on the 8th. No serious injuries were reported. School districts throughout Morris County had delayed openings while a few closed on the 7th. In Washington Township, a few back roads were closed during the early morning hours of the 8th due to icy conditions. In Jefferson, weather accounted for a rollover accident on Ridge Road during the afternoon on the 8th but resulted in no injuries. There were also six traffic accidents and a downed wire on Berkshire Valley Road. In Sussex County, about 600 customers were without power on the 7th. In the High Point and Wantage areas, ice accumulations were up to one half of an inch and coated all surfaces. This resulted in very slippery conditions and it was reported that the air temperature never got above freezing.||Some specific ice accumulation reports included; 0.50 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 0.40 inches in Alalmuchy and Blairstown (Warren County), 0.30 inches in Butler (Morris County) and Whitehouse (Hunterdon County), 0.25 inches in Newton (Sussex County), Rockaway and Lake Hopatcong (Morris County), 0.15 inches in Oak Ridge (Morris County), 0.10 inches in Mount Holly and Edgewater Park (Burlington County). ||Heavy rainfall amounts occurred and some reports included; 2.99 inches in Upper Deerfield (Cumberland County), 2.49 inches in Southampton (Burlington County), 2.28 inches in Berkeley Shore (Ocean County), 2.15 inches in Sea Girt (Monmouth County), 2.14 inches in Sicklerville (Camden County), 2.03 inches in Clayton (Gloucester County), and 1.93 inches in Lumberton (Burlington County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Whitehouse reported a total ice accumulation of 0.30 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system that moved off the Delmarva Peninsula produced light snow across southwest and central New Jersey during the morning of the 1st. Accumulations were generally less than an inch, except in Monmouth and Ocean Counties where accumulations reached around two inches. Snow began falling just after Midnight EST on the 1st and mixed with sleet at times in the southwest part of the state. The snow ended by 10 a.m. EST that morning. Precipitation in the southeast part of the state fell as mainly rain and sleet and measurable precipitation did not occur north of the Interstate 78 corridor in northern New Jersey. Because this snow occurred on a Sunday morning, very few accidents were reported.||Specific accumulations included 2.2 inches in Manasquan (Monmouth County), 2.0 inches in Brick (Ocean County) and Bradley Beach (Monmouth County), 1.9 inches in Lake Como (Monmouth County) and Point Pleasant (Ocean County), 1.5 inches in Neptune (Monmouth County), 1.0 inch in Toms River (Ocean County) and 0.9 inches in Southampton and Medford (both Burlington County). ||The light snow and sleet was caused by a rapidly moving low pressure system that moved northeast from Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the morning of February 28th to east of the Delmarva Peninsula at 7 a.m. EST on the morning of March 1st. A more significant low pressure system was about to follow for later in the day.

An intense area of low pressure over the Eastern Great Lakes on the morning of February 12, tracked northeast and up into the Canadian Maritimes by evening. High winds developed near daybreak, right behind the cold front, and continued through the day. The strongest winds occurred during the morning and appeared to be associated with sharp pressure rises right behind the cold front. ||There were about 30,000 power outages across northern New Jersey, two fatalities, and delays of up to 3 hours at Newark Liberty International airport. ||The first death occurred at approximately 0630EST as a tree fell on a car on Route 22 in Mountainside, killing the driver, a 61-year-old female; the second death occurred at approximately 1338EST, when a large tree branch fell on a 61-year-old female in Newark. ||The following are some peak wind gusts across northern New Jersey: Bergen County, 61 mph at North Arlington; Essex County, 60 mph at Newark; Hudson County, 56 mph at Jersey City; Passaic County, 48 mph at Clifton; and Union County, 53 mph at Union.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were down, resulting in power outages and road closures. Public Service Electric and Gas Co. estimated 10,000 customers were without power between Bergen and Passaic counties.

A large arctic high pressure system moved toward the area during the 16th and 17th. The extent of the arctic air mass kept maximum temperatures only in the teens, with even some single digits across northwestern New Jersey. The minimum temperatures dropped into the single digits, however some areas where the wind was able to drop off considerably, mainly in the northern part of the state, dropped to well below zero. As a storm system remained well to the northeast, the combination of this along with strong arctic high pressure to the west allowed for gusty winds. This produced wind chill values to zero and below zero for awhile, especially during the nighttime hours. The coldest wind chill value was recorded at the Sussex Airport (Sussex County) of 14 degrees below zero at 7:00 AM EST on the 17th. The majority of the state experienced wind chill values between 0 and 10 degrees below zero during early morning hours of the 16th and 17th.||The blast of arctic air from about the 14th through the 17th made the Trenton Rescue Mission brace for an influx of clients at the facility on Carroll Street. The Mission planned some action with their sister nonprofits and the city of Trenton. The Mercer County Executive���s Office issued a weather alert to residents because of the cold snap expected. An intense area of low pressure over the Eastern Great Lakes on the morning of February 12, tracked northeast and up into the Canadian Maritimes by evening. High winds developed near daybreak, right behind the cold front, and continued through the day. The strongest winds occurred during the morning and appeared to be associated with sharp pressure rises right behind the cold front. ||There were about 30,000 power outages across northern New Jersey, two fatalities, and delays of up to 3 hours at Newark Liberty International airport. ||The first death occurred at approximately 0630EST as a tree fell on a car on Route 22 in Mountainside, killing the driver, a 61-year-old female; the second death occurred at approximately 1338EST, when a large tree branch fell on a 61-year-old female in Newark. ||The following are some peak wind gusts across northern New Jersey: Bergen County, 61 mph at North Arlington; Essex County, 60 mph at Newark; Hudson County, 56 mph at Jersey City; Passaic County, 48 mph at Clifton; and Union County, 53 mph at Union.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were down, resulting in power outages and road closures. Public Service Electric and Gas Co. estimated 10,000 customers were without power between Bergen and Passaic counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary was draped from the southern Mid Atlantic region southwestward to the northern Gulf of Mexico. An area of low pressure developed along this front across the lower Mississippi Valley late in the day on the 27th. The storm system then moved northeastward and tracked near the Ohio River Valley during the morning of the 28th. As the storm continued to track northeastward, it strengthened as it moved into New England during the early evening hours of the 28th. An associated cold front then swept across the Mid Atlantic region during the early evening hours of the 28th.||A cold air mass ahead of the system allowed for snow to overspread the state generally from south to north during the 27th. As milder air then worked in especially above the surface, the snow changed to sleet and freezing rain during the night of the 27th mainly across the southern and central parts of the state. Enough warming occurred during the 28th, which allowed the wintry mix to change over to plain rain before ending mainly across the central and southern parts of the state. The mixed precipitation held on the longest across the northwestern part of the state as the cold air remained entrenched longer. The combination of the frozen and freezing precipitation along with the cold temperatures, created a high impact event for most of the state. This also produced very slippery roadways across the state.||The Hunterdon Medical Center in Raritan Township (Hunterdon County) was briefly on backup generators on the 28th due to the winter storm, which caused some problems with power lines in the area. Nineteen weather-related wrecks were reported in Warren County through 12:00 PM EST on the 28th, which included a jack-knifed tractor-trailer on a back road in Mansfield Township. The mixture of snow, sleet and rain late on the 27th and on the 28th produced messy road conditions and prompted schools to close across Morris County. Roads were particularly bad during the morning of the 28th as the snow fell at nearly an inch per hour for a time. Around 12:00 PM EST on the 28th, two lanes of Route 287 northbound were closed after a truck flipped near Exit 32 in Harding (Morris County). At about the same time, an entrance ramp on Route 80 in Mount Olive was closed because of an overturned vehicle. Less than a dozen accidents were reported in Washington Township. During the afternoon of the 28th, flooding became a problem in some areas as slush clogged storm drains and created pockets of water at intersections. An acceleration lane on Route 80 eastbound in Denville was closed briefly at 2:00 PM EST on the 28th after a large area of standing water formed. Flooding also caused some problems in Parsippany at both Baldwin and Parsippany roads. Some power outages were reported however these were mostly minimal in scope according to officials.||The winter storm produced a couple inches of snow late on the 27th and the 28th, however as milder air moved in, dense fog overspread the Ocean County area. The visibility was reduced to near zero in many areas. During the evening hours of the 28th, a fog warning was issued for the entire length of the Garden State Parkway. Due to the inclement weather, as many as 500 Jersey Central Power and Light customers lost power in the Barnegat Light area. Also, most public schools altered their schedules due to the wintry weather.||An 8-year-old girl in Woodbury (Gloucester County) was injured during the early afternoon hours of the 28th when the sled she was on collided with an SUV. The girl was sledding down a driveway and entered the street then went under the SUV, which was pulling away from the curb. The girl had to be extricated from the vehicle. In Woolwich Township (Gloucester County), the southbound lanes of the New Jersey Turnpike were blocked for about three hours during the early morning hours of the 28th after a car and a tractor-trailer collided. A female in the car was taken to a nearby hospital for serious head injuries. The State Police reported that the accident was weather related. The winter storm produced hazardous road conditions late on the 27th and on the 28th in Sussex County. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system that moved off the Delmarva Peninsula produced light snow across southwest and central New Jersey during the morning of the 1st. Accumulations were generally less than an inch, except in Monmouth and Ocean Counties where accumulations reached around two inches. Snow began falling just after Midnight EST on the 1st and mixed with sleet at times in the southwest part of the state. The snow ended by 10 a.m. EST that morning. A large arctic high pressure system moved toward the area during the 16th and 17th. The extent of the arctic air mass kept maximum temperatures only in the teens, with even some single digits across northwestern New Jersey. The minimum temperatures dropped into the single digits, however some areas where the wind was able to drop off considerably, mainly in the northern part of the state, dropped to well below zero. As a storm system remained well to the northeast, the combination of this along with strong arctic high pressure to the west allowed for gusty winds. This produced wind chill values to zero and below zero for awhile, especially during the nighttime hours. The coldest wind chill value was recorded at the Sussex Airport (Sussex County) of 14 degrees below zero at 7:00 AM EST on the 17th. The majority of the state experienced wind chill values between 0 and 10 degrees below zero during early morning hours of the 16th and 17th.||The blast of arctic air from about the 14th through the 17th made the Trenton Rescue Mission brace for an influx of clients at the facility on Carroll Street. The Mission planned some action with their sister nonprofits and the city of Trenton. The Mercer County Executive���s Office issued a weather alert to residents because of the cold snap expected. One of the shelters in West Windsor was opened on the 15th due to the arctic cold. The low temperatures on the 17th were the coldest in awhile across the region. As an example, 4 degrees was the morning low at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona (Atlantic County), which was the coldest since March 8, 2007 when it was 4 degrees. An intense area of low pressure over the Eastern Great Lakes on the morning of February 12, tracked northeast and up into the Canadian Maritimes by evening. High winds developed near daybreak, right behind the cold front, and continued through the day. The strongest winds occurred during the morning and appeared to be associated with sharp pressure rises right behind the cold front. ||There were about 30,000 power outages across northern New Jersey, two fatalities, and delays of up to 3 hours at Newark Liberty International airport. ||The first death occurred at approximately 0630EST as a tree fell on a car on Route 22 in Mountainside, killing the driver, a 61-year-old female; the second death occurred at approximately 1338EST, when a large tree branch fell on a 61-year-old female in Newark. ||The following are some peak wind gusts across northern New Jersey: Bergen County, 61 mph at North Arlington; Essex County, 60 mph at Newark; Hudson County, 56 mph at Jersey City; Passaic County, 48 mph at Clifton; and Union County, 53 mph at Union.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb struck a 61-year-old female in the head at approximately 1338EST, resulting in a fatal injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary was draped from the southern Mid Atlantic region southwestward to the northern Gulf of Mexico. An area of low pressure developed along this front across the lower Mississippi Valley late in the day on the 27th. The storm system then moved northeastward and tracked near the Ohio River Valley during the morning of the 28th. As the storm continued to track northeastward, it strengthened as it moved into New England during the early evening hours of the 28th. An associated cold front then swept across the Mid Atlantic region during the early evening hours of the 28th.||A cold air mass ahead of the system allowed for snow to overspread the state generally from south to north during the 27th. As milder air then worked in especially above the surface, the snow changed to sleet and freezing rain during the night of the 27th mainly across the southern and central parts of the state. Enough warming occurred during the 28th, which allowed the wintry mix to change over to plain rain before ending mainly across the central and southern parts of the state. The mixed precipitation held on the longest across the northwestern part of the state as the cold air remained entrenched longer. The combination of the frozen and freezing precipitation along with the cold temperatures, created a high impact event for most of the state. This also produced very slippery roadways across the state.||The Hunterdon Medical Center in Raritan Township (Hunterdon County) was briefly on backup generators on the 28th due to the winter storm, which caused some problems with power lines in the area. Nineteen weather-related wrecks were reported in Warren County through 12:00 PM EST on the 28th, which included a jack-knifed tractor-trailer on a back road in Mansfield Township. The mixture of snow, sleet and rain late on the 27th and on the 28th produced messy road conditions and prompted schools to close across Morris County. Roads were particularly bad during the morning of the 28th as the snow fell at nearly an inch per hour for a time. Around 12:00 PM EST on the 28th, two lanes of Route 287 northbound were closed after a truck flipped near Exit 32 in Harding (Morris County). At about the same time, an entrance ramp on Route 80 in Mount Olive was closed because of an overturned vehicle. Less than a dozen accidents were reported in Washington Township. During the afternoon of the 28th, flooding became a problem in some areas as slush clogged storm drains and created pockets of water at intersections. An acceleration lane on Route 80 eastbound in Denville was closed briefly at 2:00 PM EST on the 28th after a large area of standing water formed. Flooding also caused some problems in Parsippany at both Baldwin and Parsippany roads. Some power outages were reported however these were mostly minimal in scope according to officials.||The winter storm produced a couple inches of snow late on the 27th and the 28th, however as milder air moved in, dense fog overspread the Ocean County area. The visibility was reduced to near zero in many areas. During the evening hours of the 28th, a fog warning was issued for the entire length of the Garden State Parkway. Due to the inclement weather, as many as 500 Jersey Central Power and Light customers lost power in the Barnegat Light area. Also, most public schools altered their schedules due to the wintry weather.||An 8-year-old girl in Woodbury (Gloucester County) was injured during the early afternoon hours of the 28th when the sled she was on collided with an SUV. The girl was sledding down a driveway and entered the street then went under the SUV, which was pulling away from the curb. The girl had to be extricated from the vehicle. In Woolwich Township (Gloucester County), the southbound lanes of the New Jersey Turnpike were blocked for about three hours during the early morning hours of the 28th after a car and a tractor-trailer collided. A female in the car was taken to a nearby hospital for serious head injuries. The State Police reported that the accident was weather related. The winter storm produced hazardous road conditions late on the 27th and on the 28th in Sussex County. A fatal crash occurred on a snow-covered Route 206 early on the 28th as a 50-year-old man was killed when his vehicle went off the roadway.||Some snowfall totals included; 4.0 inches in Lafayette (Sussex County), 3.7 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 3.5 inches in Oak Ridge (Morris County), 3.5 inches in Whitehouse (Hunterdon County), 3.5 inches in Manalapan (Monmouth County), 3.5 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 3.4 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 3.3 inches in Oakland Mills (Monmouth County), 3.1 inches in Edison (Middlesex County), 3.1 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 3.0 inches in Somerville (Somerset County), 3.0 inches in Trenton (Mercer County), 3.0 inches in Colts Neck (Monmouth County), 2.9 inches in Pottersville (Morris County), 2.8 inches in Ewing (Mercer County), 2.8 inches in Plainsboro (Middlesex County), 2.7 inches in National Park (Gloucester County), 2.7 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 2.7 inches in Wertsville (Hunterdon County), 2.7 inches in Branchburg (Somerset County), 2.5 inches in Riegelsville (Hunterdon County), 2.3 inches in Newport (Cumberland County), 2.1 inches in Pittsgrove (Salem County), 2.0 inches in Edgewater Park (Burlington County), 2.0 inches in Stratford (Camden County), 2.0 inches in Vineland (Cumberland County), 1.9 inches in Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 1.8 inches in Somerdale (Camden County), 1.8 inches in Glassboro (Gloucester County), 1.7 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 1.5 inches in Toms River (Ocean County), 1.5 inches in West Deptford (Gloucester County), 1.5 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona (Atlantic County), 1.5 inches in Hammonton (Atlantic County), 1.2 inches 0.8 of a mile north-northwest of Woodbine (Cape May County) and 1.0 inch in Tuckerton (Ocean County).||A few ice accumulation reports included; 0.15 of an inch in Edgewater Park and Southampton, and 0.10 of an inch in Mount Laurel (all in Burlington County).

A weak low pressure system that moved off the Delmarva Peninsula produced light snow across southwest and central New Jersey during the morning of the 1st. Accumulations were generally less than an inch, except in Monmouth and Ocean Counties where accumulations reached around two inches. Snow began falling just after Midnight EST on the 1st and mixed with sleet at times in the southwest part of the state. The snow ended by 10 a.m. EST that morning. Precipitation in the southeast part of the state fell as mainly rain and sleet and measurable precipitation did not occur north of the Interstate 78 corridor in northern New Jersey. Because this snow occurred on a Sunday morning, very few accidents were reported.||Specific accumulations included 2.2 inches in Manasquan (Monmouth County), 2.0 inches in Brick (Ocean County) and Bradley Beach (Monmouth County), 1.9 inches in Lake Como (Monmouth County) and Point Pleasant (Ocean County), 1.5 inches in Neptune (Monmouth County), 1.0 inch in Toms River (Ocean County) and 0.9 inches in Southampton and Medford (both Burlington County). ||The light snow and sleet was caused by a rapidly moving low pressure system that moved northeast from Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the morning of February 28th to east of the Delmarva Peninsula at 7 a.m. EST on the morning of March 1st. A more significant low pressure system was about to follow for later in the day.

An intense area of low pressure over the Eastern Great Lakes on the morning of February 12, tracked northeast and up into the Canadian Maritimes by evening. High winds developed near daybreak, right behind the cold front, and continued through the day. The strongest winds occurred during the morning and appeared to be associated with sharp pressure rises right behind the cold front. ||There were about 30,000 power outages across northern New Jersey, two fatalities, and delays of up to 3 hours at Newark Liberty International airport. ||The first death occurred at approximately 0630EST as a tree fell on a car on Route 22 in Mountainside, killing the driver, a 61-year-old female; the second death occurred at approximately 1338EST, when a large tree branch fell on a 61-year-old female in Newark. ||The following are some peak wind gusts across northern New Jersey: Bergen County, 61 mph at North Arlington; Essex County, 60 mph at Newark; Hudson County, 56 mph at Jersey City; Passaic County, 48 mph at Clifton; and Union County, 53 mph at Union.

A weak clipper storm system moved across the area on the 15th, then strengthened some as it moved east of the New Jersey Coast. This threw additional moisture back into parts of New Jersey, which allowed 0.5 of an inch to 1.5 inches of snow to accumulate. The cold conditions combined with the snowfall and produced slippery conditions for a time on the 15th. This resulted in numerous accidents as roadways became slippery. A weak low pressure system that moved off the Delmarva Peninsula produced light snow across southwest and central New Jersey during the morning of the 1st. Accumulations were generally less than an inch, except in Monmouth and Ocean Counties where accumulations reached around two inches. Snow began falling just after Midnight EST on the 1st and mixed with sleet at times in the southwest part of the state. The snow ended by 10 a.m. EST that morning. Precipitation in the southeast part of the state fell as mainly rain and sleet and measurable precipitation did not occur north of the Interstate 78 corridor in northern New Jersey. Because this snow occurred on a Sunday morning, very few accidents were reported.||Specific accumulations included 2.2 inches in Manasquan (Monmouth County), 2.0 inches in Brick (Ocean County) and Bradley Beach (Monmouth County), 1.9 inches in Lake Como (Monmouth County) and Point Pleasant (Ocean County), 1.5 inches in Neptune (Monmouth County), 1.0 inch in Toms River (Ocean County) and 0.9 inches in Southampton and Medford (both Burlington County). ||The light snow and sleet was caused by a rapidly moving low pressure system that moved northeast from Alabama at 7 a.m. EST on the morning of February 28th to east of the Delmarva Peninsula at 7 a.m. EST on the morning of March 1st. A more significant low pressure system was about to follow for later in the day.

Strong to high winds occurred just after Midnight EST on March 2nd across eastern New Jersey as a gravity wave helped mix stronger winds aloft to the ground. Peak wind gusts reached around 60 mph in along the immediate coast. The combination of the wind and heavy snow helped cause power outages across the region. About 20,000 homes and business lost power in Atlantic Electric's service area in southeastern New Jersey and more than 2,000 in Jersey Central Power and Light's service area in Monmouth and Ocean Counties. In Cumberland County, downed trees took down four poles in Vineland. Downed wires were reported in Maurice River Township. All power was restored by 6 p.m. EST on the 2nd. The strong to high winds also caused considerable blowing and drifting of snow and made it difficult for crews to keep roads plowed and open. The high winds and waves caused minor beach erosion along the Atlantic coast.  Peak wind gusts included 60 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County) and Atlantic City (Atlantic County), 59 mph in Eldora (Cape May County), 53 mph in Millville (Cumberland County) and 48 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County). The strong to high winds were associated with the nor'easter that brought the heavy snow to the state. The nor'easter low pressure system moved from the Georgia and South Carolina border at 7 a.m. EST on the 1st to about 150 miles east of Atlantic City at 7 a.m. EST on the 2nd. The strongest winds occurred during the early morning of the 2nd as a gravity wave that emanated from the low pressure system passed across the eastern part of the state.

A cold front which followed an unseasonably warm and humid late March afternoon helped trigger severe thunderstorms mainly across central New Jersey during the evening of the 29th. All of the severe weather reports were large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped penny to quarter size hail from Mount Laurel Township east through Hainesport Township, Westampton Township, Southampton and Springfield Township. The nickel size hail in Jobstown (Springfield Township) partially covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of brush fires occurred across New Jersey on the 23rd. Most were in the northern part of the state as the combination of strong north winds, low humidity levels and a very dry March made it easy for fires to spread rapidly.||In Salem County, fifteen acres of farmland and wooded areas were consumed by a wildfire in Elsinboro Township during the afternoon. Firefighters responded to Featherbed Lane around 2 p.m. EDT after a controlled burn meant to destroy phragmites raged out of control. Ten acres of farmland were left almost completely torched as fire crews from Elsinboro and other area municipalities battled the blaze for approximately two hours. Pockets of fire continued to jump around due to strong north winds, and at one point reached into a heavily wooded area about a quarter mile from where the fire began along the banks of a small creek. Firefighters were able to douse the flames before it spread up the trees. ||In Sussex County, a brush fire affected just over ten acres near a residential community in Wantage Township. The wildfire was in a grass field adjacent to houses. Forest Fire volunteers managed to protect the homes. In Warren County, a wildfire broke out in Oxford, where five acres were burned. Other brush fires also occurred in Knowlton and Washington Townships. A minor brush fire also occurred on the afternoon of the 24th in Frelinghuysen Township. In Hunterdon County, a small wildfire burned for about 45 minutes and consumed more than an acre of farm field near Ridge Road in Kingwood Township. | |The peak wind gusts on the 23rd at both Philadelphia and Newark Liberty International Airports were 38 mph.

The combination of a low pressure system that moved off the Eastern Seaboard and an upper air disturbance that moved across from the Great Lakes, produced snow across New Jersey from the night of the 2nd into the night of the 3rd. Snowfall averaged three to six inches across much of the area, with higher amounts as much as ten inches in Gloucester County.  ||Precipitation started as light rain and light snow across the region around Midnight EST on the 3rd.  Precipitation changed to snow by dawn on the 3rd. The snow increased in intensity during the afternoon of the 3rd, but air temperatures around freezing kept much snow from accumulating on roadways. This changed as the sun was setting during the evening rush. Across the immediate Philadelphia suburbs, the snow was enhanced by an upper air disturbance that made the air mass more unstable and capable of producing snow at greater rates. The snow was falling at the rate of an inch or two per hour during the late evening. The snow ended from north to south. It ended during the evening of the 3rd in the northern half of New Jersey and by 3 a.m. EST on the 4th across the southern half of the state. It was described as a stealth snowstorm as most of the heaviest snow occurred when it had the relatively least impact on the area. ||Snow started to accumulate on untreated roadways during the evening rush and on all roadways as intensity increased later that evening. The accumulating roadway snow led to a slippery and slushy evening commute and when most of the reported accidents occurred. The snow was cited as the reason for canceling many township meetings on the evening of the 3rd. Strong and gusty south to southwest winds occurred across central New Jersey during the day on the 27th. Reported peak wind gusts reached as high as 47 mph. The statewide average precipitation of 0.64 inches was only about 20 percent of the normal February precipitation.

Strong to high winds affected New Jersey during the day on the 12th. The strong winds started shortly after a cold frontal passage between 3 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST and persisted through most of the day. Peak wind gusts averaged between 50 and 60 mph and knocked down numerous tree limbs, weak trees and power lines. Signs were knocked down, garbage cans blown around and bird feeders and toys tossed. The New Jersey Turnpike Authority banned motorcycles and car pulled trailers. About 86,000 homes and businesses lost power in the state with the strongest winds and most damage in the central and northern parts of the state. ||In Morris County, one person was injured after a fallen tree fell down and blocked a southbound lane of Interstate 287 near Harding. The southbound lane was blocked again near exit 42 after a 100 foot tree came down. In Denville, Morris Catholic High School was evacuated after foam and asphalt was blown off the gym's roof. The school was also closed the next day. Downed trees caused sections of several major roadways to be closed in the county including New Jersey State Route 53 in Parsippany, New Jersey State Route 10 in Roxbury and U.S. Route 46 in Mine Hill. A couple of parked vehicles were damaged by downed limbs at the Morris Street Jersey Transit Train Station. ||In Somerset County, morning traffic was disrupted after a downed pole closed a westbound lane of U.,S. Route 22. Farther to the west, a downed tree caused the closure of all lanes near the Hunterdon County border. All southbound lanes of Interstate 287 were closed during the late afternoon after a large tree fell on the roadway. Vehicles were damaged by downed trees in Bridgewater. In Hunterdon County, three quarters of Franklin Township lost power. In Middlesex County, a large downed tree limb damaged three vehicles in New Brunswick. In Highland Park, a downed tree caused the closure of an intersection. Another downed tree damaged a home.||In Monmouth County, a downed pole closed New Jersey State Route 34 in both directions in Wall Township. The pole also damaged the Sterns Trucking Building. In Asbury Park, late in the morning the high wind blew off a piece of the roof of a metal dipping business on First Avenue. Utility poles were split by the debris. Scaffolding broke away from a twelve story building on Deal Lake Drive. In Ocean County, a driver suffered a head injury at Mile Marker 86 on the Garden State when a tree fell on the parkway. Two other vehicles were also struck by the debris. In Toms River alone, there were about thirty weather related emergency responses between 6 a.m. EST and Noon EST. Ocean County Park in Lakewood was closed because of downed trees. ||In Mercer County, the high winds were a factor in fueling a fire that consumed two dwellings in Trenton on South Olden Avenue. Five people were left homeless. The fire was started by downed wires. Elsewhere in Trenton, roof pieces of an abandoned building (old Roebling Steel Site) were torn away as were some shingles. Hamilton Avenue was closed because of wind debris from New Jersey State Route 129 to Clark Street. In Hopewell Township, Carter Avenue was closed for three days after about half a dozen poles were snapped. A man was trapped within his dump truck until the power was cut off. In Atlantic County, Richard Stockton College was closed through the 12th because of the lack of power. A downed tree damaged a vehicle in Egg Harbor Township.    ||Peak wind gusts included 69 mph at the High Point Monument in Sussex County, 61 mph in Parsippany (Morris County), 59 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 58 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County) and Sea Girt (Monmouth County), 57 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 54 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport in Atlantic County, 53 mph in Kingwood (Hunterdon County), 52 mph in Chatsworth and Lumberton (both Burlington County), 51 mph in Somerville (Somerset County), Millville (Cumberland County), Trenton (Mercer County), Mullica (Gloucester County) and Woodstown (Salem County) and 50 mph in Deans (Middlesex County) and Blue Anchor (Camden County).  ||The strong and high winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that moved through the lower Saint Lawrence River Valley, northern New England and the Canadian Maritimes and an approaching high pressure system from the lower Mississippi Valley. The winds increased after a cold front moved through the state between 4 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST on the morning of the 12th and slowly subsided that night.

A wintry mix of snow and then freezing rain affected northwest New Jersey from the afternoon of the 18th into the early morning of the 19th. Snow began during the afternoon of the 18th. The snow changed over to rain in the valleys during the evening of the 18th, but changed over to freezing rain over some of the higher terrain locations. Precipitation ended during the early morning of the 19th. Snowfall averaged around an inch and ice accretions were less than one-tenth of an inch. ||Untreated roadways were treacherous, especially during the evening commute. Several accidents occurred across the higher terrain. For example in Jefferson Township (Morris County), nine accidents were reported and included one overturned vehicle.||The wintry mix was caused by a low pressure that moved northeast from the Ohio Valley through the Saint Lawrence Valley on the night of the 18th. Most of the snow occurred preceding this low pressure system's warm front while precipitation ended after its cold front moved through the region overnight.

Strong to high winds affected New Jersey during the day on the 12th. The strong winds started shortly after a cold frontal passage between 3 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST and persisted through most of the day. Peak wind gusts averaged between 50 and 60 mph and knocked down numerous tree limbs, weak trees and power lines. Signs were knocked down, garbage cans blown around and bird feeders and toys tossed. The New Jersey Turnpike Authority banned motorcycles and car pulled trailers. About 86,000 homes and businesses lost power in the state with the strongest winds and most damage in the central and northern parts of the state. ||In Morris County, one person was injured after a fallen tree fell down and blocked a southbound lane of Interstate 287 near Harding. The southbound lane was blocked again near exit 42 after a 100 foot tree came down. In Denville, Morris Catholic High School was evacuated after foam and asphalt was blown off the gym's roof. The school was also closed the next day. Downed trees caused sections of several major roadways to be closed in the county including New Jersey State Route 53 in Parsippany, New Jersey State Route 10 in Roxbury and U.S. Route 46 in Mine Hill. A couple of parked vehicles were damaged by downed limbs at the Morris Street Jersey Transit Train Station. ||In Somerset County, morning traffic was disrupted after a downed pole closed a westbound lane of U.,S. Route 22. Farther to the west, a downed tree caused the closure of all lanes near the Hunterdon County border. All southbound lanes of Interstate 287 were closed during the late afternoon after a large tree fell on the roadway. Vehicles were damaged by downed trees in Bridgewater. In Hunterdon County, three quarters of Franklin Township lost power. In Middlesex County, a large downed tree limb damaged three vehicles in New Brunswick. In Highland Park, a downed tree caused the closure of an intersection. Another downed tree damaged a home.||In Monmouth County, a downed pole closed New Jersey State Route 34 in both directions in Wall Township. The pole also damaged the Sterns Trucking Building. In Asbury Park, late in the morning the high wind blew off a piece of the roof of a metal dipping business on First Avenue. Utility poles were split by the debris. Scaffolding broke away from a twelve story building on Deal Lake Drive. In Ocean County, a driver suffered a head injury at Mile Marker 86 on the Garden State when a tree fell on the parkway. Two other vehicles were also struck by the debris. In Toms River alone, there were about thirty weather related emergency responses between 6 a.m. EST and Noon EST. Ocean County Park in Lakewood was closed because of downed trees. ||In Mercer County, the high winds were a factor in fueling a fire that consumed two dwellings in Trenton on South Olden Avenue. Five people were left homeless. The fire was started by downed wires. Elsewhere in Trenton, roof pieces of an abandoned building (old Roebling Steel Site) were torn away as were some shingles. Hamilton Avenue was closed because of wind debris from New Jersey State Route 129 to Clark Street. In Hopewell Township, Carter Avenue was closed for three days after about half a dozen poles were snapped. A man was trapped within his dump truck until the power was cut off. In Atlantic County, Richard Stockton College was closed through the 12th because of the lack of power. A downed tree damaged a vehicle in Egg Harbor Township.    ||Peak wind gusts included 69 mph at the High Point Monument in Sussex County, 61 mph in Parsippany (Morris County), 59 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 58 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County) and Sea Girt (Monmouth County), 57 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 54 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport in Atlantic County, 53 mph in Kingwood (Hunterdon County), 52 mph in Chatsworth and Lumberton (both Burlington County), 51 mph in Somerville (Somerset County), Millville (Cumberland County), Trenton (Mercer County), Mullica (Gloucester County) and Woodstown (Salem County) and 50 mph in Deans (Middlesex County) and Blue Anchor (Camden County).  ||The strong and high winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that moved through the lower Saint Lawrence River Valley, northern New England and the Canadian Maritimes and an approaching high pressure system from the lower Mississippi Valley. The winds increased after a cold front moved through the state between 4 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST on the morning of the 12th and slowly subsided that night.

Strong and gusty south to southwest winds occurred across central New Jersey during the day on the 27th. Reported peak wind gusts reached as high as 47 mph. Gusty west to northwest winds affected higher terrain and coastal locations of New Jersey from the afternoon of the 22nd into the afternoon of the 23rd. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph. It was the driest February on record at both the Atlantic City International Airport (0.68 inches) and Trenton (0.64 inches). The statewide average precipitation of 0.64 inches was only about 20 percent of the normal February precipitation.

The combination of a low pressure system that moved off the Eastern Seaboard and an upper air disturbance that moved across from the Great Lakes, produced snow across New Jersey from the night of the 2nd into the night of the 3rd. Snowfall averaged three to six inches across much of the area, with higher amounts as much as ten inches in Gloucester County.  ||Precipitation started as light rain and light snow across the region around Midnight EST on the 3rd.  Precipitation changed to snow by dawn on the 3rd. The snow increased in intensity during the afternoon of the 3rd, but air temperatures around freezing kept much snow from accumulating on roadways. This changed as the sun was setting during the evening rush. Across the immediate Philadelphia suburbs, the snow was enhanced by an upper air disturbance that made the air mass more unstable and capable of producing snow at greater rates. The snow was falling at the rate of an inch or two per hour during the late evening. The snow ended from north to south. It ended during the evening of the 3rd in the northern half of New Jersey and by 3 a.m. EST on the 4th across the southern half of the state. It was described as a stealth snowstorm as most of the heaviest snow occurred when it had the relatively least impact on the area. ||Snow started to accumulate on untreated roadways during the evening rush and on all roadways as intensity increased later that evening. The accumulating roadway snow led to a slippery and slushy evening commute and when most of the reported accidents occurred. The snow was cited as the reason for canceling many township meetings on the evening of the 3rd. Strong and gusty south to southwest winds occurred across central New Jersey during the day on the 27th. Reported peak wind gusts reached as high as 47 mph. The strong winds caused a static line to break in South Amboy (Middlesex County). This caused about 6,300 homes, businesses and municipal offices in South Amboy and Sayreville to lose power for about one hour. Peak wind gusts included 47 mph in Trenton (Mercer County) and 40 mph at both the Atlantic City Marina and Newark Liberty International Airport. The strong winds occurred in the southerly flow that preceded a cold front.||While there were a couple of wind events during the month of February, there was not much precipitation in New Jersey. The statewide average precipitation of 0.64 inches was only about 20 percent of the normal February precipitation.

Strong and gusty south to southwest winds occurred across central New Jersey during the day on the 27th. Reported peak wind gusts reached as high as 47 mph. The strong winds caused a static line to break in South Amboy (Middlesex County). This caused about 6,300 homes, businesses and municipal offices in South Amboy and Sayreville to lose power for about one hour. Peak wind gusts included 47 mph in Trenton (Mercer County) and 40 mph at both the Atlantic City Marina and Newark Liberty International Airport. The strong winds occurred in the southerly flow that preceded a cold front.||While there were a couple of wind events during the month of February, there was not much precipitation in New Jersey. It was the driest February on record at both the Atlantic City International Airport (0.68 inches) and Trenton (0.64 inches).  On a statewide average, it was also the driest February on record dating back to 1895. The statewide average precipitation of 0.64 inches was only about 20 percent of the normal February precipitation.

Strong gusty west winds followed a cold front passage across the New Jersey from the afternoon of the 19th into the afternoon of the 20th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph.||In Eatontown (Monmouth County), the strong winds contributed to the collapse of the vacant Eatontown Roller Rink on New Jersey State 35 and Clinton Avenue on the night of the 19th. The roof and sidewalls of the building partially collapsed. The building was scheduled to be demolished. No injuries were reported.||Peak wind gusts included 50 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 44 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport, 43 mph in Bivalve (Cumberland County) and 42 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County). The gusty winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that went slowly northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system that approached from the Mississippi Valley.
