Strong to high winds affected New Jersey during the day on the 12th. The strong winds started shortly after a cold frontal passage between 3 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST and persisted through most of the day. Peak wind gusts averaged between 50 and 60 mph and knocked down numerous tree limbs, weak trees and power lines. Signs were knocked down, garbage cans blown around and bird feeders and toys tossed. The New Jersey Turnpike Authority banned motorcycles and car pulled trailers. About 86,000 homes and businesses lost power in the state with the strongest winds and most damage in the central and northern parts of the state. ||In Morris County, one person was injured after a fallen tree fell down and blocked a southbound lane of Interstate 287 near Harding. The southbound lane was blocked again near exit 42 after a 100 foot tree came down. In Denville, Morris Catholic High School was evacuated after foam and asphalt was blown off the gym's roof. The school was also closed the next day. Downed trees caused sections of several major roadways to be closed in the county including New Jersey State Route 53 in Parsippany, New Jersey State Route 10 in Roxbury and U.S. Route 46 in Mine Hill. A couple of parked vehicles were damaged by downed limbs at the Morris Street Jersey Transit Train Station. ||In Somerset County, morning traffic was disrupted after a downed pole closed a westbound lane of U.,S. Route 22. Farther to the west, a downed tree caused the closure of all lanes near the Hunterdon County border. All southbound lanes of Interstate 287 were closed during the late afternoon after a large tree fell on the roadway. Vehicles were damaged by downed trees in Bridgewater. In Hunterdon County, three quarters of Franklin Township lost power. In Middlesex County, a large downed tree limb damaged three vehicles in New Brunswick. In Highland Park, a downed tree caused the closure of an intersection. Another downed tree damaged a home.||In Monmouth County, a downed pole closed New Jersey State Route 34 in both directions in Wall Township. The pole also damaged the Sterns Trucking Building. In Asbury Park, late in the morning the high wind blew off a piece of the roof of a metal dipping business on First Avenue. Utility poles were split by the debris. Scaffolding broke away from a twelve story building on Deal Lake Drive. In Ocean County, a driver suffered a head injury at Mile Marker 86 on the Garden State when a tree fell on the parkway. Two other vehicles were also struck by the debris. In Toms River alone, there were about thirty weather related emergency responses between 6 a.m. EST and Noon EST. Ocean County Park in Lakewood was closed because of downed trees. ||In Mercer County, the high winds were a factor in fueling a fire that consumed two dwellings in Trenton on South Olden Avenue. Five people were left homeless. The fire was started by downed wires. Elsewhere in Trenton, roof pieces of an abandoned building (old Roebling Steel Site) were torn away as were some shingles. Hamilton Avenue was closed because of wind debris from New Jersey State Route 129 to Clark Street. In Hopewell Township, Carter Avenue was closed for three days after about half a dozen poles were snapped. A man was trapped within his dump truck until the power was cut off. In Atlantic County, Richard Stockton College was closed through the 12th because of the lack of power. A downed tree damaged a vehicle in Egg Harbor Township.    ||Peak wind gusts included 69 mph at the High Point Monument in Sussex County, 61 mph in Parsippany (Morris County), 59 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 58 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County) and Sea Girt (Monmouth County), 57 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 54 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport in Atlantic County, 53 mph in Kingwood (Hunterdon County), 52 mph in Chatsworth and Lumberton (both Burlington County), 51 mph in Somerville (Somerset County), Millville (Cumberland County), Trenton (Mercer County), Mullica (Gloucester County) and Woodstown (Salem County) and 50 mph in Deans (Middlesex County) and Blue Anchor (Camden County).  ||The strong and high winds were caused by the pressure difference between an intensifying low pressure system that moved through the lower Saint Lawrence River Valley, northern New England and the Canadian Maritimes and an approaching high pressure system from the lower Mississippi Valley. The winds increased after a cold front moved through the state between 4 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST on the morning of the 12th and slowly subsided that night.

A winter storm occurred across most of New Jersey from the late afternoon of the 1st into the late afternoon of the 2nd. About six to twelve inches of snow fell across the region, with some locally higher amounts near the coast and lower amounts in Mercer, Somerset and Sussex Counties. Many snow emergencies were declared. The combination of strong winds and heavy snow made travel difficult at best and made it tough for road crews to keep thoroughfares opened. New Jersey State Police reported more than 500 crashes throughout the state from the start of the event through 330 p.m. EST on the 2nd. There were many fender benders, spin-outs and slides into other vehicles. The Mid-Atlantic AAA responded to 2,600 calls for assistance throughout their region. All schools as well as many county and municipal offices and businesses, restaurants and factories were closed on Monday March 2nd. Even Rutgers and Rowan Universities were closed. New Jersey state workers had a two hour delayed opening and early dismissal on the 2nd. Clean-up from the storm was estimated to cost the state about seven million dollars. Many meetings and sporting events were postponed on the 2nd. Many New Jersey Transit buses and trains were delayed. Schools did not reopen until the 3rd in Salem and Cumberland Counties and not until the 4th in Monmouth County. | |Snow began across the southeast part of the state during the late afternoon on the 1st and spread into the northern part of the state by late in the evening. The snow fell heavy at times during the evening and overnight as gravity wave bands of heavier snow moved through the eastern part of the state. Snow of generally lighter intensity fell during the day on the 2nd, before it ended during the late afternoon.| |In Somerset County, about twenty accidents occurred on Interstates 287 and 78. In Mercer County, about 15 crashes occurred on Interstates 95, 195 and 205. In Burlington County, a jackknifed tractor trailer closed two southbound lanes of Interstate 295 around 10 a.m. EST on the 2nd. In Camden County, a Brinks Truck overturned on New Jersey State Route 42 in Blackwood. In Gloucester County, a truck accident on U.S. Route 322 wrecked the front yard of a home in Harrison Township. In Atlantic County, twenty-three accidents were reported on the Atlantic City Expressway. In Cumberland County, 70 accidents were reported and included a disabled truck that blocked the Vineland exit of New Jersey State Route 55. In Cape May County, a head on collision on the George Redding Bridge caused injuries. ||Snowfall totals included 13.5 inches in Colts Neck (Monmouth County), 13.0 inches in Wall (Monmouth County) and Bayville (Ocean County), 12.9 inches in Vineland (Cumberland County), 12.5 inches in Freehold (Monmouth County) and Barnegat Light (Ocean County), 12.4 inches in Northfield (Atlantic County), 11.5 inches in Malaga (Gloucester County), 11.3 inches in Atlantic City (Atlantic County), 11.0 inches in Point Pleasant (Ocean County), 10.6 inches in Hopatcong (Sussex County), 10.5 inches in New Lisbon (Burlington County), Eldora (Cape May County), Marcella (Morris County) and Whiting (Ocean County), 9.8 inches in Califon (Hunterdon County), 9.6 inches in Hammonton (Atlantic County), 9.5 inches in Seaville (Cape May County), 9.4 inches in Cream Ridge (Monmouth County), 9.0 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County) and Woodbine (Cape May County), 8.9 inches in Stewartsville (Warren County), 8.5 inches in Millville (Cumberland County) and Butler (Morris County), 8.3 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County), 7.2 inches in Sicklerville (Camden County), 6.9 inches in White House Station (Hunterdon County), 6.8 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport, 6.7 inches in North Plainfield (Somerset County), 6.5 inches in Sewell (Gloucester County) and New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 6.1 inches in Parsippany (Morris County), 6.0 inches in West Cape May (Cape May County) and Newton (Sussex County), 5.6 inches in Maple Shade (Burlington County), 5.5 inches in Metuchen (Middlesex County), 5.2 inches in Somerdale (Camden County), 5.1 inches in Ewing (Mercer County) and 5.0 inches in Hamilton Square (Mercer County) and Hillsborough (Somerset County).||The winter storm was caused by a nor'easter that moved east of the region. A coastal low pressure system along the Outer Banks of North Carolina Sunday evening, March 1, tracked northeast, to east of Long Island by Monday afternoon, March 2.  At the same time, arctic high pressure, from the upper midwest to James Bay, was gradually building east.  The two combined to produce a heavy snowfall event across the tri-state area with snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches an hour during the pre-dawn hours of March 2. ||Snowfall amounts across northeast New Jersey generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches, but as high as 13.5 inches. The combination of weak onshore flow and some of the highest spring tides of the year because of lunar perigee coinciding with the new moon caused minor to moderate tidal flooding during the late afternoon and evening high tide cycle from June 20th through June 25th along the New Jersey coast. The highest tides occurred during the evening and night of the 22nd when some moderate tidal flooding was reported.||Tidal flooding caused a few road closures along the coast. These included New Jersey State Route 47 leading into Wildwood (Cape May County) and a few roads in Manasquan (Monmouth County). ||The highest tides included 7.86 feet above mean lower low water in South Amboy (Middlesex County), 7.79 feet above mean lower low water in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 7.37 feet above mean lower low water in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 7.02 feet above mean lower low water (Atlantic County), 6.88 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May Harbor (Cape May County) and 7.71 feet above mean lower low water at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (Cape May County).  Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.70 feet above mean lower low water and moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.70 feet above mean lower low water.||Minor tidal flooding extended into Delaware Bay and tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries north through Burlington County.  Tides reached 7.75 feet above mean lower low water at Reedy Island (New Castle County); minor tidal flooding begins at 7.2 feet above mean lower low water. The high tide reached 9.06 feet above mean lower low water in Philadelphia; minor tidal flooding begins at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water and moderate tidal flooding begins at 9.2 feet above mean lower low water.

Thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a warm front during the morning hours across portions of northeastern New Jersey. These storms produced lightning damage in Passaic county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was struck by lightning on 44th Street, resulting in minor structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave rotating around a deep upper trough over New England acted on warm humid air in place at the surface to allow for supercell thunderstorms to form and track across Northeast New Jersey.  These storms were prolific hail producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickle size hail was reported in Plainfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms preceding a cold front moved across New Jersey during the afternoon of the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a large tree in Kingwood Township onto County Route 519. A few other large tree limbs were torn down in the township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms moved across northern New Jersey during the early morning on the 9th. A more organized squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved through southern New Jersey later that morning. The squall line formed near a warm frontal boundary. In addition to the winds, the thunderstorms were prolific lightning producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped dime to nickel size hail in West Collingswood Borough. Pea size hail fell in nearby Mount Ephraim Borough.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm pulled down large tree limbs and wires in Evesham Township.

A slow southward moving cold front located over the Leatherstocking Region of New York State during the morning of June 2nd helped trigger severe thunderstorms in southern New Jersey during that afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced wind gusts to around 60 mph near the Delaware Memorial Bridge in Pennsville Township. Trees were also knocked down in the township. Wind damage from the severe thunderstorm extended east into Quinton Township where a couple of two foot diameter trees were knocked down. Atlantic City Electric reported about 5,300 homes and businesses lost power in Salem County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow southward moving cold front located over the Leatherstocking Region of New York State during the morning of June 2nd helped trigger severe thunderstorms in southern New Jersey during that afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees and tree limbs in Vineland. There were ten major roadways with downed trees in the city. Most of the wind damage occurred south of Chestnut Avenue and east of the Boulevard.  Large fallen trees were reported on Garry Avenue and South Lincoln Street. A downed tree damaged the roof, garage and porch of a home on Blackberry Court. A vehicle was damaged by a downed tree. The gust front from the same severe thunderstorm produced estimated wind gusts of 60 mph in Millville. There were three reports of downed trees. Vineland Electric and Atlantic City Electric reported about 7,300 homes and businesses lost power in the county. Power was restored by midnight EDT that night.

A wildfire consumed about 315 acres of woodland in Atlantic County. The fire began about 2 p.m. EDT on the 18th. It started near the former Jersey Devil cabin off of Bremen Avenue in Egg Harbor City within the Pinelands National Reserve. The fire briefly caused some evacuations including one woman with respiratory problems. The fire spread east and jumped Bremen Avenue and spread to Frankfurt Avenue in Galloway Township. Portions of a white cedar tree stand burned. The fire was half contained by 630 p.m. EDT on the 18th, seventy-five percent contained by the night of the 19th and was deemed under control at 8 a.m. EDT on the 20th. The fire consumed about 315 acres of the Atlantic County white cedar swamp within the Pinelands. More than sixty firefighters from ten separate fire companies battled the blaze. The peak wind gust at the Atlantic City International Airport on the 18th was 26 mph from the westnorthwest.

An approaching cold front helped trigger a line of thunderstorms that moved through New Jersey during the evening of the 21st. There were numerous reports of small hail as the line moved through Camden, Sussex, Warren, Morris, Hunterdon and Somerset Counties. Larger hail fell in Burlington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and ensuing fire badly damaged a garage in Woodbury Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger a line of thunderstorms that moved through New Jersey during the evening of the 21st. There were numerous reports of small hail as the line moved through Camden, Sussex, Warren, Morris, Hunterdon and Somerset Counties. Larger hail fell in Burlington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported penny size hail in Southampton Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was submerged in flood waters at the intersection of Shepards Lane and Union Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger thunderstorms during the night of July 1st that produced both heavy downpours and frequent lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a pine tree in the front yard of a Katherine Drive home in Rockaway Township and caused it to explode about 4 a.m. EDT. The tree shrapnel impaled a nearby home's roof and damaged the back window of a car. The cable connection within the home was fried by the lightning strike. All power was restored that afternoon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped hail up to the size of quarters in Hamilton Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger thunderstorms during the night of July 1st that produced both heavy downpours and frequent lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding of the Beaver Brook in downtown Clinton during the early morning of the 2nd. Leigh and Main Streets flooded around 4 a.m. EDT. The back of five buildings were flooded on Leigh Street. A restaurant on East Main Street suffered minimal flood damage. Two police vehicles were also flooded and damaged. The flood waters receded by 6 a.m. EDT and all roads were reopened. Storm totals from northern Hunterdon County included 4.78 inches in Califon, 3.27 inches in Annandale and 3.11 inches in High Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front produced strong to severe thunderstorms in central New Jersey during the early evening of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a large tree across Forked Neck Road in Shamong Township. A vehicle veered to miss the falling the tree and ended in a ditch. No injuries were reported. A couple of other large tree limbs were pulled down in the township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of the 28th in northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down onto the roof of a house during a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of the 28th in northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires in East Brunswick Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered but slow moving thunderstorms with heavy rain affected southern New Jersey during the afternoon on the 13th and caused isolated flooding. The thunderstorms formed near an inverted trof boundary that extended north from a low pressure system in North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain in southern Somerset County and northern Mercer County caused flooding along the Millstone River in Griggstown. The Giriggstown Causeway was closed. The Millstone River at Giriggstown was above its 10 foot flood stage from 1203 a.m. EDT on the 14th through 157 a.m. EDT on the 15th. It crested at 10.75 feet at 245 p.m. EDT on the 14th. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 1.5 and 2.0 inches. While not the heaviest rain, the first half of June and the month of June as a whole was wetter than normal and made it easier for streams and creeks to potentially flood.||The heavier than normal rain that fell throughout the month of June made it the sixth wettest June on record (6.61 statewide average) for New Jersey since 1895. The heavy rain made it difficult to make hay, for farmers to get into wet fields, caused fruits to swell, drowned some crops and increased the number of slugs, mosquitoes, mold and mushrooms. It also cut down on the number of visitors to pick your own stands and wreaked havoc on the schedules of youth leagues and other outdoor events. ||For individual stations, it was the third wettest June on record for Atlantic City (7.05 inches). June also established a new record for the most number of days with measurable precipitation (17 days). In Trenton, it was the ninth wettest June on record (6.96 inches) and also set a record for the most number of days with measurable precipitation (21 days).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and humid air mass combined with an approaching surface trof to produce strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening across New Jersey. The combination of lightning and damaging winds caused about 20,000 homes and businesses to lose power in Hunterdon, Somerset, Middlesex, Monmouth and Union Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a rotating wall cloud and funnel cloud over Frenchtown Borough. While the funnel cloud did not touch down, the severe thunderstorm went on to produce wind and hail damage in Hunterdon County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front arriving from the west late in the day on the 2nd moved into a warm and moist air mass, which resulted in thunderstorms across the area with torrential rainfall. Hunterdon County reported the worst damage. Flooding occurred from Mercer and Hunterdon Counties northeast through Morris and Middlesex Counties. Doppler Radar storm total estimates average 1.5 to 3.0 inches across the region, most of which fell within one hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours damaged a home in Hamilton Township, flooded and closed U.S. Route 206 in Lawrence Township and caused flooding along the Assunpink Creek. In Hamilton Township, a home on Evergreen Avenue became structurally unsound after its basement foundation collapsed. Several other homes in the area also had flooded basements. In Lawrence Township, U.S. Route 206 was flooded near Notre Dame High School. The Assunpink Creek at Trenton was above its 8.5 foot flood stage from 312 p.m. EDT through 801 p.m. EDT on the 2nd. It crested at 8.96 feet at 630 p.m. EDT. Event totals included 3.54 inches in Lawrence Township, 2.51 inches in Hamilton Township and 2.15 inches in Hightstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front arriving from the west late in the day on the 2nd moved into a warm and moist air mass, which resulted in thunderstorms across the area with torrential rainfall. Hunterdon County reported the worst damage. Flooding occurred from Mercer and Hunterdon Counties northeast through Morris and Middlesex Counties. Doppler Radar storm total estimates average 1.5 to 3.0 inches across the region, most of which fell within one hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding of smaller streams and led to flooding along the North Branch of the Raritan River as well as parts of the Millstone River. Several roads were flooded and closed. In Bridgewater Township, Chambers Brook flooded. Several roads in the township were closed and a few water rescues were performed. Roads were also flooded and closed in Manville, Bedminster and Bound Brook.  Event precipitation totals included 3.52 inches in Pottersville, 1.92 inches in Bridgewater, 1.90 inches in Bound Brook, 1.71 inches in Bridgewater, 1.68 inches in Somerville and 1.48 inches in Bernards Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused considerable poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of some of the smaller streams in eastern Morris County. Roadways had as much as five feet of water on them from Morris Plains northeast through Parsippany. One motorist was trapped in their car. Storm totals included 2.48 inches in Morris Township, 2.37 inches in Mendham Township, 2.26 inches in Parsippany and 1.90 inches in Boonton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved into the area late on the 21st, which ran into a very warm and humid air mass. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front from west to east. Locally torrential rainfall occurred, which resulted in areas of flash flooding especially during the night time hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding in Hi-Nella Borough.  Minnetonka Road was closed after three feet of water accumulated on it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved into the area late on the 21st, which ran into a very warm and humid air mass. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front from west to east. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain that fell from the night of the 21st into the 22nd caused flooding along sections of the Millstone River and road closures such as the Griggstown Causeway. The Millstone River at Griggstown was above its 10 foot flood stage from 853 a.m. EDT on the 22nd through 625 a.m. EDT on the 24th. It crested at 14.47 feet at 245 a.m. EDT on the 23rd. Two day event precipitation totals included 4.72 inches in Belle Mead, 3.80 inches in Bound Brook, 3.38 inches in Hillsborough and 2.81 inches in Bridgewater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of what once was Tropical Storm Danny passed east of the New Jersey Coast during the day on the 29th. In spite of this enhanced rip currents and heavy surf  pounded the New Jersey coast for the second consecutive weekend (the 29th and 30th). Waves reached as high as 8 feet and bathing restrictions were in place. A surfer in Belmar (Monmouth County) broke his leg in the rough surf. While some beach erosion occurred, it was not as severe as the previous weekend associated with Hurricane Bill.

At the airport, a plane was thrown across a roadway into a building. Large sections of steel corrugated roofing was ripped from a few hangers. A mobile home office was destroyed. Several small planes were pushed into each other. In Ewing Township, five homes on Sandy Lane were damaged by downed trees. One family was displaced. A wind gust of 64 mph was recorded at Mercer County Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning and the ensuing fires destroyed two homes on Jacobs Creek Road and Stevens Oaks Lane within Ewing Township. No injuries were reported, but two dogs and a cat perished within the home on Jacobs Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that moved through the state late Friday afternoon helped produce strong to severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon of the 31st. Most of the wind damage occurred as a line of severe thunderstorms known as a bow echo moved across the southern half of the state. About 67,000 homes and businesses lost power, 45,000 within the service area of Atlantic City Electric in the southeastern part of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another strong microburst from a severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees in northeastern Ocean County including Toms River (Dover Township) and in the Beechwood and Bayville sections of Berkeley Township. In Berkeley Township, there 100 weather related calls to emergency responders. Many roads were closed by downed trees. A VFW building suffered wind damage. Wind damage in Toms River was concentrated around Windsor Avenue. A wind gust of 59 mph was measured in Brick Township and a wind gust of 60 mph was measured at Seaside Heights.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms knocked down trees and wires throughout Gloucester County. A downed tree damaged a home in West Deptford Township. A downed tree also fell onto Interstate 295 in Logan Township. A downed tree damaged a vehicle in Wenonah Borough and at the Deptford Mall in Deptford Township. Several trees and poles were knocked down in East Greenwich Township and forced the closure of a few roadways. There were about 300 weather related calls throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of thunderstorms with heavy rain and recent wet weather conditions produced some flash flooding across the state from mid afternoon into early evening on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm with heavy downpours caused small stream flooding as well as roadway flooding in and around Stratford Borough. The heavily traveled White Horse Pike (U.S. Route 30) was closed after up to two feet of water accumulated on it. Flooding occurred along the South Branch of the Cooper River. In Haddonfield, flooding caused damage to books within the Haddonfield Public Library. About 360 books were damaged beyond repair in the children's library. The library's carpeting was also damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms downed trees, tree limbs and wires in Moorestown, Willingboro and Florence Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of high pressure was located off the East Coast on the 10th, a hot and very humid air mass (dew points in the mid to even locally upper 70s) was across the area. This resulted in heat indices at above the century mark, with the heat index at Atlantic City peaking at 104 degrees. Most high temperatures on the coastal plain were in the mid 90s and for many places this was the hottest day of 2009. A cold front moved through that night and ended the brief spell of hot weather. High temperatures included 96 degrees in Cherry Hill (Camden County), 95 degrees in Hillsborough (Somerset County) and New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 94 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport, Sea Girt (Monmouth County) and Eastampton (Burlington County) and 93 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County), Woodstown (Salem County) and Woodbine (Cape May County).

As an area of high pressure was located off the East Coast on the 10th, a hot and very humid air mass (dew points in the mid to even locally upper 70s) was across the area. This resulted in heat indices at above the century mark, with the heat index at Atlantic City peaking at 104 degrees. Most high temperatures on the coastal plain were in the mid 90s and for many places this was the hottest day of 2009. A cold front moved through that night and ended the brief spell of hot weather. High temperatures included 96 degrees in Cherry Hill (Camden County), 95 degrees in Hillsborough (Somerset County) and New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 94 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport, Sea Girt (Monmouth County) and Eastampton (Burlington County) and 93 degrees in Trenton (Mercer County), Woodstown (Salem County) and Woodbine (Cape May County).

A frontal boundary in the area along with a weak area of low pressure combined to produce areas of showers and thunderstorms on the 12th, mainly in the afternoon and evening hours. The showers and thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall, which resulted in areas of flooding during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary thunderstorm dropped a Doppler Radar estimated 4 to 5 inches over a small section of Robbinsville Township. The ensuing flooding caused the closure of U.S. Route 130 in the township as well as the backing of water against about ten homes along Charlene Court.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front arriving from the west late in the day on the 2nd moved into a warm and moist air mass, which resulted in thunderstorms across the area with torrential rainfall. Hunterdon County reported the worst damage. Flooding occurred from Mercer and Hunterdon Counties northeast through Morris and Middlesex Counties. Doppler Radar storm total estimates average 1.5 to 3.0 inches across the region, most of which fell within one hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused considerable flash flooding of smaller streams and led to flooding along the South Branch of the Raritan River. Numerous roads were flooded and three bridges had to be closed. Hardest hit were Clinton and Tewksbury Township. ||In Clinton, the Beaver Brook flooded for the second time this summer. It flooded Main Street, East Main Street, Lehigh Street and Old Highway 22. Some stores and their basements were flooded. The flooding brook carried benches, newspaper bins, garbage dumpsters and even a couple of cars. More than two dozen basements were flooded. In Clinton Township, Camp Carr was evacuated. In Tewksbury Township, the Rockaway Creek (which exceeded its banks by 100 feet) flooded basements on Water Street.  In Califon Borough, basement flooding as flooding along Main Street and Old Califon Road occurred. In Readington Township, along the South Branch of the Raraitan River, flooding was reported in the Stanton area.||The biggest public property toll occurred to three bridges in the northern part of the county. In Tewksbury Township, the Water Street Bridge between Longview Road and County Route 639 was expected to be closed for repairs for up to five months. The Guinea Hollow Road Bridge was expected to be closed for up to 10 weeks after a wall collapsed. In Lebanon Tonwship, the Buffalo Hollow Road Bridge between Poplar Road and New Jersey State Route 31 was also closed for about three weeks.||Event precipitation totals included 4.35 inches in Califon, 3.62 inches in Annandale, 3.51 inches in High Bridge, 3.45 inches in Clinton and 2.48 inches in Flemington.

An area of high pressure on the 18th was off the Mid Atlantic coast, while a cold front was well off to the west. In between these two systems was a surface trough, which helped to focus showers and thunderstorms within a warm and humid air mass. The thunderstorms, some severe, marched across the region during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires in Cherry Hill. One tree fell onto and damaged a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving warm front during the morning of the 9th, produced some showers and thunderstorms. As the region became entrenched in the warm sector during the day, this helped to ignite a line of thunderstorms that affected southern New Jersey during the early evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Logan Township, including one onto Interstate 295. No injuries were reported. About 1,000 homes and businesses lost power in Gloucester County because of downed trees and tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary in the area along with a weak area of low pressure combined to produce areas of showers and thunderstorms on the 12th, mainly in the afternoon and evening hours. The showers and thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall, which resulted in areas of flooding during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused multiple roads to flood as well as flooding of smaller streams in and around eastern parts of Egg Harbor Township and Northfield. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 3 to 4 inches within Atlantic County centered around Egg Harbor Township. Actual precipitation totals included 2.25 inches in Estell Manor and 1.90 inches in Northfield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down large tree limbs and wires in Blackwood (Gloucester Township).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms knocked down several trees and power lines mainly across the western half of Salem County. In Mannington Township, the wind ripped an historic cupola on top of the historic yellow barn on a farm on Kings Highway. About 3,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving warm front during the morning of the 9th, produced some showers and thunderstorms. As the region became entrenched in the warm sector during the day, this helped to ignite a line of thunderstorms that affected southern New Jersey during the early evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down numerous trees and wires across Cumberland County. About 5,800 homes and businesses lost power. Hardest hit were Bridgeton and Hopewell Township. A wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at the Millville Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward moving warm front during the morning of the 9th, produced some showers and thunderstorms. As the region became entrenched in the warm sector during the day, this helped to ignite a line of thunderstorms that affected southern New Jersey during the early evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of large tree limbs and wires in Monroe Township. A house was also struck by lightning, but reported no damage. About 1,000 homes and businesses lost power in Gloucester County because of downed trees and tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary in the area along with a weak area of low pressure combined to produce areas of showers and thunderstorms on the 12th, mainly in the afternoon and evening hours. The showers and thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall, which resulted in areas of flooding during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused considerable poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of some smaller streams in Hamilton and Egg Harbor Townships. Some major roadway flooding was reported on New Jersey State Route 50, U.S. Route 322, Pomona Road and Somers Point-Mays Landing Road. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary settled southward across most of the area during the 28th before stalling nearby. Plenty of moisture flowing northward along with incoming energy resulted in some showers and thunderstorms, and some storms were locally strong to severe during the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms with damaging winds moved across Cumberland. Several trees and wires were knocked down from Millville to Bridgeton.

A powerful nor'easter produced wind gusts to nearly 60 mph, widespread moderate tidal flooding, heavy rain and severe beach erosion along the New Jersey coast from November 12th through the 14th. Initial damage estimates were placed at 180 million dollars. By several measures this was one of the worst nor'easters to affect New Jersey since 1990. The Dolan Davis Nor'easter power ranking for Long Island Buoy 44025 ranked it 4th and the strongest since March of 1994. The Miller Storm Erosion Index and the Kraus and Wise Maximum Wave Run-up Index were both ranked second only to December 1992 nor'easter. The highest winds occurred from the afternoon of the 12th into the afternoon of the 13th. Several thousand people lost power. The heaviest rain fell on the 12th. The highest tides occurred with the morning high tide on the 13th except in Monmouth and Middlesex Counties where they occurred with the morning high tide on the 14th. This was the highest tides in central and southern New Jersey since either 1998 or 1996. Tidal departures reached up to four feet. Governor Jon Corzine declared a state of emergency in Atlantic, Burlington, Cape May, Cumberland, Ocean and Monmouth Counties on November 15th.||Cape May County reported the worst damage. A State of Emergency was declared on the 12th. There were no mandatory evacuations, but about 100 people left their homes in Ocean City and Wildwood. Shelters were opened in Avalon, Lower Township and North Wildwood. During the morning high tide on the 13th, nearly every bridge and causeway leading to the barrier islands were closed. Several of them were also closed on the 12th and 14th. The Townsend Inlet Bridge between Sea Isle City and Avalon was closed through the 15th after a work barge rammed into it. Motorists were rescued from flood waters in Avalon. The business districts in Avalon, Sea Isle City and Stone Harbor were all flooded. Homes in Sea Isle City also had homes that suffered first floor flooding. Significant erosion was reported in Ocean City, Sea Isle City (three dune breaches) and Avalon. In Ocean City along damage was expected to exceed 90 million dollars. The city lost 7 million cubic yards of sand. There was no beach left north of 21st Street in Avalon. The high winds damaged the roofs of three motels in Wildwood along with one large restaurant sign. Service on the Cape May Lewes Ferry was limited. ||In Atlantic County, flooding caused the closure of the Black Horse (U.S. Routes 40 and 322) and  White Horse (U.S. Route 30) Pikes near Atlantic City on the 12th and 13th. This caused huge traffic delays on the Atlantic City Expressway. Motels were also evacuated along the Black and White Horse Pikes. Dozens of motorists were rescued from flood waters along the White Horse Pike and U.S. Route 9. The high surf and coastal flooding caused about 10 million dollars in damage to the Atlantic City boardwalk and beach. In Atlantic City and also Ventnor, ramps to the beach and boardwalk were destroyed. Overall county damage was estimated at 16 million dollars.   ||In Ocean County, foundations to homes were exposed and threatened in Beach Haven on Cape May, Atlantic and Mercer Avenues in Harvey Cedars. New Jersey State Route 72, the route off and on Long Beach Island, the Dorland J. Henderson Memorial Bridge was closed around high tide on the 13th. Dunes were wrecked and bulkheads exposed on Long Beach Island. Damage on Long Beach Island was estimated at 30 million dollars. Waves breaking on Long Beach Island were measured at 9 feet. Most of the beach at Island Beach State Park was deeply eroded. The high winds blew the sand onto roadways adjacent to beaches and caused very large drifts in Seaside Park. In Monmouth County, Sea Bright was hit the hardest by the flooding. New Jersey State Route 35 and many side streets were flooded. The coastal flooding tides also caused flooding adjacent to the Navasink, Shrewsbury and Shark Rivers in Monmouth Beach, Long Branch, Belmar and Manasquan. Elsewhere tidal flooding was minor within Delaware Bay and tidal sections of the Delaware River. Some streets were flooded in Downe Township (Cumberland County).|   |The high tide reached 8.21 feet above mean lower low water in Cape May (Cape May County), the highest tide since the February 5, 1998 nor'easter. The high tide reached 7.57 feet above mean lower low water in Atlantic City (Atlantic County), the highest tide since March 19, 1996 nor'easter. The high tide reached 7.84 feet above mean lower low water in Sandy Hook. A higher tide occurred with the recent nor'easter on October 18th. ||Other peak tides included 8.33 feet above mean lower low water in South Amboy (Middlesex County), 8.35 feet above mean lower low water in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 6.79 feet above mean lower low water in Sea Bright (Monmouth County), 7.7 feet above mean lower low water in Belmar (Monmouth County), 7.41 feet above mean lower low water in Absecon (Atlantic County), 7.53 feet above mean lower low water in Margate (Atlantic County), 7.62 feet above mean lower low water in Ocean City (Cape May County), 7.96 feet above mean lower low water in Sea Isle City (Cape May County), 7.69 feet above mean lower low water in Avalon (Cape May County), 8.49 feet above mean lower low water in Bivalve (Cumberland County), 7.65 feet above mean lower low water in Greenwich (Cumberland County), 7.78 feet above mean lower low water on Reedy Island (New Castle County, Delaware) and 8.70 feet above mean lower low water in Philadelphia.  ||Peak wind gusts included 59 mph at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County) and Barnegat (Ocean County), 57 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), 55 mph in Tuckerton (Ocean County), 54 mph in Avalon (Cape May County) and Brant Beach and Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 52 mph in Seaside Heights (Ocean County) and Ocean City (Cape May County), 51 mph in Wildwood and 47 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport. ||The nor'easter was originally Hurricane Ida, which made landfall in Alabama on the morning of the 10th. As Ida made landfall, it lost tropical characteristics and transformed into an extratropical low pressure system. On the morning of November 11th, the low pressure system reached southern Georgia and the next morning (12th) it was located over the coastal waters east of Wilmington, North Carolina. Of equal importance, was a high pressure system that moved across New England and the Canadian Maritimes from the 12th through the 14th. Its effects were threefold, it increased the difference in surface pressure (thus increased the winds), it blocked the low pressure system from moving offshore and provided a very long fetch of onshore flow. The extratropical low pressure system reached its maximum strength of around 995 millibars during the evening of the 12th as it was located just off of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina. From there, the low pressure system slowly drifted off the North Carolina coast as it slowly weakened on the 13th and 14th.

Its effects were threefold, it increased the difference in surface pressure (thus increased the winds), it blocked the low pressure system from moving offshore and provided a very long fetch of onshore flow. The extratropical low pressure system reached its maximum strength of around 995 millibars during the evening of the 12th as it was located just off of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina. From there, the low pressure system slowly drifted off the North Carolina coast as it slowly weakened on the 13th and 14th.

A cold front arriving from the west late in the day on the 2nd moved into a warm and moist air mass, which resulted in thunderstorms across the area with torrential rainfall. Hunterdon County reported the worst damage. Flooding occurred from Mercer and Hunterdon Counties northeast through Morris and Middlesex Counties. Doppler Radar storm total estimates average 1.5 to 3.0 inches across the region, most of which fell within one hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the torrential downpours, especially in Mercer and Hunterdon Counties caused river flooding that lasted into August 4th. The Millstone River at Griggstown was above its 10 foot flood stage from 550 p.m. EDT on the 2nd through 115 a.m. EDT on the 4th. It crested at 11.96 feet at 745 a.m. EDT on the 3rd. The North Branch of the Raritan River at North Branch was above its 12.3 foot flood stage from 653 p.m. EDT through 1108 p.m. EDT on the 2nd. It crested at 13.04 feet at 915 p.m. EDT. Farther downstream at its confluence point, the North Branch of the Raritan River at the village of South Branch was above its 7 foot flood stage from 444 p.m. EDT on the 2nd through 610 a.m. EDT on the 3rd. It crested at 8.77 feet at 2 a.m. EDT on the 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front arriving from the west late in the day on the 2nd moved into a warm and moist air mass, which resulted in thunderstorms across the area with torrential rainfall. Doppler Radar storm total estimates average 1.5 to 3.0 inches across the region, most of which fell within one hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain in Hunterdon County led to flooding along the sections of the South Branch of the Raritan River. The South Branch of the Raritan River at Stanton was above its 8 foot flood stage from 153 p.m. EDT on the 2nd through 211 p.m. EDT on the 3rd. It crested at 11.17 feet at 5 p.m. EDT on the 2nd.

An approaching cold front produced strong to severe thunderstorms across extreme southeast New Jersey during the late afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped penny size hail in Erma in Lower Township and nickel to quarter size hail in Green Creek, Mayville, Rio Grande and Whitesboro in Middle Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front triggered thunderstorms during the night of the 11th in Ocean County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oyster Creek Nuclear Generating Station was forced to shut power generation down after lightning strikes in the Lacey Township area caused outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and humid air mass combined with an approaching surface trof to produce strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening across New Jersey. The combination of lightning and damaging winds caused about 20,000 homes and businesses to lose power in Hunterdon, Somerset, Middlesex, Monmouth and Union Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm left a swath of wind damage across the northern third of Somerset County. Numerous trees and power lines were knocked down. In Bedminster Township, the shingles and half the roof of a supermarket were torn away on Hills Drive. Trees were knocked down on both County Routes 313 and 113. A tree was also knocked down onto Interstate 287. Thirteen vehicles and four condominiums were damaged by downed trees in the township.  In Bridgewater Township, U.S. Route 202/206 was closed after a downed tree pulled down wires onto the roadway.  In Bernards Township, on Mountain Road, a downed tree tore a hole in the roof of one home and also damaged its deck. In Warren Township, downed trees damaged homes on Sage Drive and Church Road. A vehicle was also damaged.  A wind gust of 55 mph was recorded at the Somerville Somerset Airport. Some small hail also fell from the severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and humid air mass combined with an approaching surface trof to produce strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening across New Jersey. The combination of lightning and damaging winds caused about 20,000 homes and businesses to lose power in Hunterdon, Somerset, Middlesex, Monmouth and Union Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused wind damage (downed trees and wires) from Freehold Township and Borough northeast into Middletown Township. Wind damage also occurred in Marlboro and Manalapan Townships. A downed tree pierced a roof in the Port Monmouth section of Middletown Township. Three vehicles were damaged by fallen trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a few trees across southwestern Mercer County in Trenton and Hamilton Township. Two fifty foot trees were completely uprooted in Hamilton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of thunderstorms with heavy rain and recent wet weather conditions produced some flash flooding across the state from mid afternoon into early evening on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding and flooding of smaller streams in extreme southern Warren County in sections of Phillipsburg, Alpha and Pohatcong Township. Flooding was also reported on Interstate 78 in Phillipsburg. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 2 and 2.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front produced strong to severe thunderstorms across extreme southeast New Jersey during the late afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm blew over lifeguard stands, overturned portable toilets and tossed life guard boats down the beach in Wildwood. A measured wind gust of 58 mph was reported at the Cape May Naval Observing Station.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and ensuing fire damaged twelve units of a condominium on Fisher Drive in Franklin Township. Six of the units were deemed uninhabitable due to fire, smoke and water damage. One resident was hospitalized after she became light-headed. It took about 60 firefighters two hours to get the fire under control. Damages were estimated.

The combination of lightning and damaging winds caused about 20,000 homes and businesses to lose power in Hunterdon, Somerset, Middlesex, Monmouth and Union Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down numerous trees in northwestern Burlington County. Downed trees were reported in both Bordentowns, Chesterfield, Fieldsboro, Mansfield and Springfield. Seven roadways were blocked by downed trees and limbs fell on and damaged several homes and porches as well as at least one vehicle. About 2,000 homes and businesses lost power. Power was fully restored by 4 p.m. EDT on the 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second cold frontal passage in as many days helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms across the northern half of New Jersey during the evening of the 17th. Thousands of homes and businesses lost power. There were still about 1,000 homes and businesses without power in the hardest hit counties of Hunterdon, Morris and Somerset Counties on the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees in several additional municipalities in central Morris County. Downed trees were reported in Dover, Hanover, Montville, Morris Plains, Parsippany and Wharton. There were still 180 people without power in the county on the afternoon of the 18th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused wind damage across Hunterdon County, hitting the central part of the county the hardest in Alexandria, Franklin and Clinton Townships. Ten people suffered minor injuries at the Quick Chek New Jersey Festival of Ballooning in Readington Township when tents were toppled and debris struck them. In Alexandria Township, the roof of a barn was torn away and a shed was totally damaged. Tree damage occurred in Clinton along New Jersey State Route 31 and County Route 929. About 5,000 homes and businesses lost power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred around the Widing River in Toms River (Dover Township). Some motorists were rescued from trapped vehicles on New Jersey State Route 37 near Hospital Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of thunderstorms with heavy rain and recent wet weather conditions produced some flash flooding across the state from mid afternoon into early evening on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding within Long Branch. A few motorists were rescued from trapped vehicles. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger thunderstorms during the night of July 1st that produced both heavy downpours and frequent lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the heavy rain earlier on the morning of the 2nd across northern Hunterdon County led to minor flooding along the South Branch of the Raritan River. The South Branch of the Raritan River at Stanton was above its 8 foot flood stage from 918 a.m. EDT through 1151 a.m. EDT on the 2nd. It crested at 8.19 feet at 1015 a.m. EDT. Farther upstream, the South Branch of the Raritan River at High Bridge crested just below (9.94 feet) its 10 foot flood stage at 4 a.m. EDT. Storm totals included 4.78 inches in Califon, 3.27 inches in Annandale and 3.11 inches in High Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that developed along a stationary frontal boundary east of New Jersey brought strong northeast winds to coastal areas during the day on the 23rd. The northeast winds combined with unusually high spring tides associated with the new moon to help cause widespread minor tidal flooding at the time of the high tide on the evening of the 23rd. Some minor tidal flooding started as early as the evening of the 19th, but the highest tides and the most widespread minor tidal flooding occurred on the 23rd. Along the ocean front the tides on the 23rd reached 7.58 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County), 7.50 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and 7.05 feet above mean lower low water at Atlantic City. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water and moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water. The higher than normal tides extended into Delaware Bay and tidal sections of the Delaware River during the night of the 23rd and also caused minor tidal flooding. The high tide at Reedy Island (New Castle County Delaware) reached 7.39 feet above mean lower low water (minor tidal flooding starts at 7.2 feet above mean lower low water) and in Philadelphia reached 8.37 feet above mean lower low water (minor tidal flooding starts at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water).

A warm front retreating to the north and a cold front approaching from the west produced strong to severe thunderstorms across New Jersey during the afternoon and early evening of the 29th. One EF-2 tornado also occurred. The wind damage and lightning resulted in about 57,000 homes and businesses in losing power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado touched down in Wantage Township at about 248 p.m. EDT on the 29th. It was the first confirmed tornado in Sussex County since August of 1990, the first tornado of F2 or EF2 strength ever in the county since records started in 1950 and the first tornado to reach EF2 or F2 strength in New Jersey since the Manalapan tornado of May 27, 2001. The tornado remained on the ground for 6.6 miles before it crossed the border into New York State. Its maximum width was about 100 yards and its highest estimated wind speed was 120 mph. The tornado damaged thousands of trees, decimated acres of farmland and some rural property.||The tornado touched down near the intersection of Route 519 and Rutgers Road, then crossed Libertyville Road, Snoyer Road and Ramsey Road, producing minor tree and limb damage along the way.  More substantial damage, mainly in the form of downed trees and some minor structural damage, occurred after the tornado crossed New Jersey State Route 23 near its intersection with Unionville Road and Rose Morrow Road. The worst damage of the entire tornado occurred along Beemer Road and on the north side of New Jersey State Route 23.  Substantial damage occurred to the Ricker Farm, as two barns and one silo were destroyed. Two other barns suffered severe wind damage. Some minor damage also occurred to the adjacent farmhouse. Pieces of one barn roof were found three quarters of a mile away. A two week old calf was killed by the flying debris, two others were thrown fifty feet, but not seriously hurt. Damage estimates for the Ricker farm reached as high as $500,000. ||Hundreds of trees were felled further north along Beemer Road, and power was out for several hours as power lines were brought down by the tornado and falling trees.  The tornado weakened after it crossed Wolfpit Road and Black Dirt Road on its way into New York State. On Wolfpit Road, it ripped the porch from a home and lifted a boat and carried it one quarter of a mile away.  A vineyard in the township was also damaged. The tornado crossed into Orange County, New York near Quarry Road as an EF-1.  No other deaths or injuries were reported from this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nor'easter brought an early season measurable snow to the higher terrain of Northwest New Jersey from the  afternoon of the 15th into the morning of the 16th. Accumulations on the higher terrain averaged one to four inches. ||Rain began falling during the morning of the 15th and mixed with or changed to snow over the higher terrain as a heavier precipitation burst moved across the region during the afternoon. This precipitation burst coincided with the start of evening commute and accidents occurred along the Interstate 80 corridor. Precipitation became lighter during the evening and overnight and changed back to rain in most places except for the higher terrain of Sussex County. Precipitation ended during the morning of the 16th. By then temperatures were above freezing.||Because the ground was still warm, most of the accumulations occurred on non paved surfaces, bridges and overpasses as well as clinging to the trees and leaves. A few accidents were reported.  Northeast winds averaged 10 to 20 mph.  This was strong enough to remove some of the snow off the trees and power outages were generally isolated.   ||Specific accumulations included 4.0 inches in Vernon (Sussex County), 2.7 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 2.2 inches in Randolph (Morris County), 2.0 inches in Blairstown (Warren County) and 1.0 inch in Knowlton (Warren County), Sparta (Sussex County) and Roxbury (Morris County). ||The nor'easter that caused the snow moved from the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 14th to the northern North Carolina coastal waters on the morning of the 15th. It then moved northeast and by sunrise on the 16th was already southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts.

Event precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 3.0 inches with the heaviest rain occurring across Mercer, Hunterdon, Somerset, Middlesex and Morris Counties. This caused flash flooding during the first half of the evening and runoff flooding into the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain during the evening of the 24th caused flooding along sections of the Millstone River in Somerset County. The Millstone River at Griggstown was above its 10 foot flood stage from 233 a.m. EDT through 1052 p.m. EDT on the 25th. Event precipitation totals in Somerset County included 2.60 inches in Bound Brook, 2.42 inches in Pottersville, 2.39 inches in Hillsborough, 2.32 inches in Neshanic and Belle Mead, 2.13 inches in Montgomery and 1.66 inches in Blackwells Mills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west to northwest winds occurred across New Jersey from the middle of the morning into the early evening on the 7th. Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph and knocked down some weak trees, tree limbs and wires. One girl was injured by flying debris in Mercer County and several roads in Morris and Middlesex Counties  were closed. About 16,700 homes and businesses in the Jersey Central Power & Light, Public Service Electric & Gas and Atlantic City Electric service areas lost power.||A young girl was injured in Trenton (Mercer County) after debris blew off a roof. In Middlesex County, in Metuchen, four vehicles were damaged by fallen trees. A street light was knocked down in Sayreville. In Morris County, parts of New Jersey State Route 53 were closed because of downed trees. In Gloucester County, in Woodbury, a house's roof was damaged by a downed trees. There were dozens of weather related calls in the county mainly in Logan, Deptford and Monroe Townships. In Mercer County, the strong winds damaged beyond repair a 200-year-old American Beech Tree in Lawrenceville. The speed limit on the Delaware Memorial Bridge was lowered from 50 mph to 25 mph.| |Peak wind gusts included 56 mph in Atlantic City (Atlantic County), 55 mph at the Newark Liberty International Airport in Essex County, 51 mph in Seaside Heights (Ocean County), 50 mph in Woodstown (Salem County), 47 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County) and Cape May (Cape May County), 46 mph in Hillsborough (Somerset County) and Millville (Cumberland County),  45 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport, Pittstown (Hunterdon County) and Burlington (Burlington County) and 43 mph at the Mercer County Airport.||The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between a low pressure system (around 990 millibars) that was moving northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system moving from the Central Plains toward the area.  As the high pressure system moved closer, winds diminished during the evening of the 7th.

A pair of low pressure systems that emerged from the Gulf Of Mexico and passed southeast of New Jersey on the 27th and 28th gave the state nearly 48 hours of steady rain from the evening of the 26th into the afternoon of the 28th. Event precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 3 inches with the highest amounts in the southeast part of the state. The heaviest rain fell during the late morning on the 27th and again during the first half of the day on the 28th as the low pressure systems made their closest passes to New Jersey. The heavy rain caused flooding on parts of the Millstone River in Somerset County as well as some of the more typical poor drainage flooding in the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain caused flooding along sections of the Millstone River on the 28th and 29th. The Millstone River at Griggstown was above its 10 foot flood stage from 1152 a.m. EDT on the 28th through 755 p.m. EDT on the 29th. It crested at 11.39 feet at 1 a.m. EDT on the 29th. Event precipitation totals included 1.87 inches in Bridgewater Township, 1.86 inches in Bedminster, 1.79 inches in Franklin Township, 1.77 inches in Somerville,  1.72 inches in Hillsborough Township and 1.65 inches in Bernards Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the first nor'easter made its closest pass to the New Jersey coast during the early evening on the 15th, northeast winds increased with peak gusts reaching around 45 mph from Monmouth County southward. A few trees were knocked down in Ocean ans Monmouth Counties. While the northeast winds subsided, the direction persisted into the 18th and helped cause tidal flooding.  Peak wind gusts on the 15th included 46 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 45 mph in Seaside Heights (Ocean County) and 40 mph in Cape May (Cape May County).

As the first nor'easter made its closest pass to the New Jersey coast during the early evening on the 15th, northeast winds increased with peak gusts reaching around 45 mph from Monmouth County southward. A pair of nor'easters caused minor to moderate tidal flooding along the ocean and  Delaware Bay from the evening high tide of the 15th into the morning high tide of the 19th and minor tidal flooding along the Delaware River from the morning of the 16th into the early afternoon of the 17th. Tidal flooding extended into the inlets and back bays. In addition to the tidal flooding, heavy surf contributed to and exacerbated the erosion along the ocean and lower Delaware Bay. Several major roadways were flooded and closed.| |Along the ocean and lower Delaware Bay, minor tidal flooding began with the afternoon high tide on the 15th. Moderate tidal flooding then occurred with the morning high tides on the 16th through the 18th. There was also pockets of moderate tidal flooding with the evening high tide on the 17th. ||In Monmouth County, roadways were closed in Monmouth Beach, Sea Bright and Manasquan. In Ocean County, on Long Beach Island, a couple of miles of Long Beach Boulevard were closed. Other roads were also closed in Ship Bottom and Manahawkin. In Manahawkin, pilings debris closed East Bay Avenue. In Atlantic County, roads were closed along the back bays in Egg Harbor Township, Galloway, Ventnor and West Atlantic City. In Cape May County, a state of emergency was declared on the 16th. On the 16th, in Sea Isle City, a school bus with 15 students on board had to be rescued from flood waters. The National Guard was deployed to help with a few evacuations in Avalon, Lower Township, Ocean City, Stone Harbor, West Wildwood and Wildwood. Major roadway closures due to tidal flooding included the 9th Street Bridge (New Jersey State Route 52) to Ocean City, the Townsends Inlet Bridge between Sea Isle City and Avalon and the George Reading Bridge (New Jersey State Route 47) between Middle Township and Wildwood. In Avalon, by the Townsends Inlet Bridge an overwash of the seawall at Ocean Drive caused 20 feet of road shoulder to collapse on the west side of the roadway. Other road closures were reported in Sea Isle City and Strathmere. Four feet of water accumulated on some roadways in West Wildwood. ||The immediate shore took an erosion pounding from several days of northeast winds. In Raritan Bay, minor erosion occurred in Old Bridge, Union Beach, Keansburg, Bayshore and Port Monmouth. Along the ocean in Monmouth County, vertical cuts averaged 3 to 6 feet, but reached 8 feet in both Sea Bright and Asbury Park. In Ocean County, vertical cuts also average 3 to 6 feet with many municipalities reporting damage to dune fencing.  But, vertical cuts reached up to 9 feet in Point Pleasant, up to 10 feet in Island Beach State Park (with a dune cut near Fisherman's Walkway), up to 10 feet in Loveladies, up to 13 feet in Harvey Cedars, up to 10 feet in North Beach, and up to 8 feet in Long Beach Township, Brant Beach, Beach Haven and Holgate.  In Beach Haven, minor washover occurred at Merivale Avenue. in Holgate, an access road was washed out in the Holgate Natural Area. In Atlantic County, vertical cuts averaged 2 feet, but reached 6 feet in Ventnor. In Cape May County, vertical cuts averaged 1 to 3 feet, but there were wide variations reported within townships. In Ocean City, an 8 by 14 foot cut in the dune occurred from Pennyland Avenue north to the 9th Street Bridge. Geotubes were exposed along Surf Avenue. In Sea Isle City, vertical cuts reached 6 feet at the south end of the municipality. In Avalon, vertical cuts reached up to 12 feet between 11th and 26th Streets. In Stone Harbor, a 6 foot by 10 foot dune cut occurred from 100th through 115th Streets. In Cape May City, a 10 foot high by 6 foot wide dune cut occurred for 300 feet at Wilmington Avenue. Minor beach erosion also occurred on the Delaware Bay side of Cape May County.| |In Cape May County, the highest tides occurred with the morning high tide on the 17th. Elsewhere along the ocean, the highest tides occurred with the morning high tide on the 18th. In Cape May (Cape May County) at the Ferry Terminal, the tide reached 8.16 feet above mean lower low water. This was the highest tide since February 5, 1998. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water.  In Atlantic City (Atlantic County), the tide reached 7.20 feet above mean lower low water. This was the highest tide since October 7, 2006. Moderate tidal flooding begins at 7.0 feet above mean lower low water. In Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), the tide reached 8.21 feet above mean lower low water. This was the highest tide since March 19, 1996. Moderate tidal flooding begins at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water. ||Along tidal sections of the Delaware River into Burlington County, minor tidal flooding occurred from the late morning of the 16th into the early afternoon of the 17th. The highest tides occurred with the late morning high tide on the 16th. At Reedy Island, (New Castle County, Delaware) the tide reached 7.42 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding begins at 7.20 feet above mean lower low water. In Philadelphia, Pennsylvania the tide reached 8.23 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding begins at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.||The first nor'easter moved from the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 14th to the northern North Carolina coastal waters on the morning of the 15th and then moved rapidly northeast and by sunrise on the 16th was already southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts. The second nor'easter formed off the Delmarva coastal waters on the 17th and drifted very slowly east farther offshore as it intensified slowly. It was located about 250 miles east of Cape May, New Jersey during the afternoon of the 18th and then accelerated to the northeast and passed through Nova Scotia on the morning of the 19th. As these nor'easters formed and moved offshore, a high pressure system remained over nearby Canada and northern New England; both combined to maintain the onshore flow.

As the first nor'easter made its closest pass to the New Jersey coast during the early evening on the 15th, northeast winds increased with peak gusts reaching around 45 mph from Monmouth County southward. A few trees were knocked down in Ocean ans Monmouth Counties. While the northeast winds subsided, the direction persisted into the 18th and helped cause tidal flooding.  Peak wind gusts on the 15th included 46 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 45 mph in Seaside Heights (Ocean County) and 40 mph in Cape May (Cape May County).

Gusty west to northwest winds occurred across New Jersey from the middle of the morning into the early evening on the 7th. Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph and knocked down some weak trees, tree limbs and wires. One girl was injured by flying debris in Mercer County and several roads in Morris and Middlesex Counties  were closed. About 16,700 homes and businesses in the Jersey Central Power & Light, Public Service Electric & Gas and Atlantic City Electric service areas lost power.||A young girl was injured in Trenton (Mercer County) after debris blew off a roof. In Middlesex County, in Metuchen, four vehicles were damaged by fallen trees. A street light was knocked down in Sayreville. In Morris County, parts of New Jersey State Route 53 were closed because of downed trees. In Gloucester County, in Woodbury, a house's roof was damaged by a downed trees. There were dozens of weather related calls in the county mainly in Logan, Deptford and Monroe Townships. In Mercer County, the strong winds damaged beyond repair a 200-year-old American Beech Tree in Lawrenceville. The speed limit on the Delaware Memorial Bridge was lowered from 50 mph to 25 mph.| |Peak wind gusts included 56 mph in Atlantic City (Atlantic County), 55 mph at the Newark Liberty International Airport in Essex County, 51 mph in Seaside Heights (Ocean County), 50 mph in Woodstown (Salem County), 47 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County) and Cape May (Cape May County), 46 mph in Hillsborough (Somerset County) and Millville (Cumberland County),  45 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport, Pittstown (Hunterdon County) and Burlington (Burlington County) and 43 mph at the Mercer County Airport.||The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between a low pressure system (around 990 millibars) that was moving northeast through the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system moving from the Central Plains toward the area.  As the high pressure system moved closer, winds diminished during the evening of the 7th.

A pair of nor'easters caused minor to moderate tidal flooding along the ocean and  Delaware Bay from the evening high tide of the 15th into the morning high tide of the 19th and minor tidal flooding along the Delaware River from the morning of the 16th into the early afternoon of the 17th. Tidal flooding extended into the inlets and back bays. In addition to the tidal flooding, heavy surf contributed to and exacerbated the erosion along the ocean and lower Delaware Bay. Several major roadways were flooded and closed.| |Along the ocean and lower Delaware Bay, minor tidal flooding began with the afternoon high tide on the 15th. Moderate tidal flooding then occurred with the morning high tides on the 16th through the 18th. There was also pockets of moderate tidal flooding with the evening high tide on the 17th. ||In Monmouth County, roadways were closed in Monmouth Beach, Sea Bright and Manasquan. In Ocean County, on Long Beach Island, a couple of miles of Long Beach Boulevard were closed. Other roads were also closed in Ship Bottom and Manahawkin. In Manahawkin, pilings debris closed East Bay Avenue. In Atlantic County, roads were closed along the back bays in Egg Harbor Township, Galloway, Ventnor and West Atlantic City. In Cape May County, a state of emergency was declared on the 16th. On the 16th, in Sea Isle City, a school bus with 15 students on board had to be rescued from flood waters. The National Guard was deployed to help with a few evacuations in Avalon, Lower Township, Ocean City, Stone Harbor, West Wildwood and Wildwood. Major roadway closures due to tidal flooding included the 9th Street Bridge (New Jersey State Route 52) to Ocean City, the Townsends Inlet Bridge between Sea Isle City and Avalon and the George Reading Bridge (New Jersey State Route 47) between Middle Township and Wildwood. In Avalon, by the Townsends Inlet Bridge an overwash of the seawall at Ocean Drive caused 20 feet of road shoulder to collapse on the west side of the roadway. Other road closures were reported in Sea Isle City and Strathmere. Four feet of water accumulated on some roadways in West Wildwood. ||The immediate shore took an erosion pounding from several days of northeast winds. In Raritan Bay, minor erosion occurred in Old Bridge, Union Beach, Keansburg, Bayshore and Port Monmouth. Along the ocean in Monmouth County, vertical cuts averaged 3 to 6 feet, but reached 8 feet in both Sea Bright and Asbury Park. In Ocean County, vertical cuts also average 3 to 6 feet with many municipalities reporting damage to dune fencing.  But, vertical cuts reached up to 9 feet in Point Pleasant, up to 10 feet in Island Beach State Park (with a dune cut near Fisherman's Walkway), up to 10 feet in Loveladies, up to 13 feet in Harvey Cedars, up to 10 feet in North Beach, and up to 8 feet in Long Beach Township, Brant Beach, Beach Haven and Holgate.  In Beach Haven, minor washover occurred at Merivale Avenue. in Holgate, an access road was washed out in the Holgate Natural Area. In Atlantic County, vertical cuts averaged 2 feet, but reached 6 feet in Ventnor. In Cape May County, vertical cuts averaged 1 to 3 feet, but there were wide variations reported within townships. In Ocean City, an 8 by 14 foot cut in the dune occurred from Pennyland Avenue north to the 9th Street Bridge. Geotubes were exposed along Surf Avenue. In Sea Isle City, vertical cuts reached 6 feet at the south end of the municipality. In Avalon, vertical cuts reached up to 12 feet between 11th and 26th Streets. In Stone Harbor, a 6 foot by 10 foot dune cut occurred from 100th through 115th Streets. In Cape May City, a 10 foot high by 6 foot wide dune cut occurred for 300 feet at Wilmington Avenue. Minor beach erosion also occurred on the Delaware Bay side of Cape May County.| |In Cape May County, the highest tides occurred with the morning high tide on the 17th. Elsewhere along the ocean, the highest tides occurred with the morning high tide on the 18th. In Cape May (Cape May County) at the Ferry Terminal, the tide reached 8.16 feet above mean lower low water. This was the highest tide since February 5, 1998. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water.  In Atlantic City (Atlantic County), the tide reached 7.20 feet above mean lower low water. This was the highest tide since October 7, 2006. Moderate tidal flooding begins at 7.0 feet above mean lower low water. In Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), the tide reached 8.21 feet above mean lower low water. This was the highest tide since March 19, 1996. Moderate tidal flooding begins at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water. ||Along tidal sections of the Delaware River into Burlington County, minor tidal flooding occurred from the late morning of the 16th into the early afternoon of the 17th. The highest tides occurred with the late morning high tide on the 16th. At Reedy Island, (New Castle County, Delaware) the tide reached 7.42 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding begins at 7.20 feet above mean lower low water. In Philadelphia, Pennsylvania the tide reached 8.23 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding begins at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.||The first nor'easter moved from the Tennessee Valley on the morning of the 14th to the northern North Carolina coastal waters on the morning of the 15th and then moved rapidly northeast and by sunrise on the 16th was already southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts. The second nor'easter formed off the Delmarva coastal waters on the 17th and drifted very slowly east farther offshore as it intensified slowly. It was located about 250 miles east of Cape May, New Jersey during the afternoon of the 18th and then accelerated to the northeast and passed through Nova Scotia on the morning of the 19th. As these nor'easters formed and moved offshore, a high pressure system remained over nearby Canada and northern New England; both combined to maintain the onshore flow.
